# `internal/engine` — deep dive for the `translate serve` HTTP adapter

## Package shape (no sub-packages)

`internal/engine` is a **single flat Go package** — there are no sub-packages under it. All 12 non-test source files share `package engine`:

`engine.go` (core types/interface), `chain.go`, `llm.go`, `dict.go`, `localdict.go`, `cedict.go`, `ecdict.go`, `dictdata.go`, `google.go`, `smartauto.go`, `smartdict.go`, `models.go`, `prompt.go`, `errors.go`. The package imports zero TUI code (it depends only on `internal/lang`, `internal/debug`, `internal/xdgpath`, and two third-party libs: `modernc.org/sqlite`, `github.com/agnivade/levenshtein`). The engine layer does **not** import `internal/store` — history persistence lives entirely at the call site (see §7).

---

## 1. Public API surface

### The central abstraction — `Engine` interface (`internal/engine/engine.go:201`)

```go
type Engine interface {
	Name() string
	Translate(ctx context.Context, req Request) (<-chan Chunk, error)
	Detect(ctx context.Context, text string) (string, error)
	Available(ctx context.Context) bool
	Supports(m Mode) bool
}
```

Every method takes/honors `context.Context`. There is a **single entry point for all work — `Translate`** — parameterized by `Request.Mode` (translate vs dict). There is no separate `Define`/`Detect`-heavy surface (see §3).

Two secondary interfaces:
- `ModelLister` (`llm.go:203`): `Models(ctx context.Context) ([]string, error)` — implemented by `*LLMEngine`.

### Modes and request/result types (`engine.go`)

```go
type Mode int
const ( ModeTranslate Mode = iota; ModeDict )   // engine.go:14

type Request struct {          // engine.go:23
	Text          string
	Source        string  // BCP-47-ish or "auto"/""
	Target        string  // "en", "zh", ...
	Mode          Mode
	MaxAlts       int
	Stream        bool    // caller wants token streaming (LLM only)
	Model         string  // per-request model override
	ModelProvider string  // scopes Model to one provider
	Preset        string  // "" => concise
	Extra         string  // extra user instructions appended to system prompt
	Pair          bool    // bidirectional pair mode
	PairHome      string
	PairAway      string
	Learn         bool    // structured tutor mode (always non-streaming)
	Bilingual     bool    // whole-doc mode (always non-streaming)
	Segments      []Segment
}

type Segment struct { Text string; Code bool }   // engine.go:65
```

```go
type TranslateResult struct {   // engine.go:72 — also the history-record shape
	Translation     string   `json:"translation"`
	DetectedSource  string   `json:"detected_source,omitempty"`
	Target          string   `json:"target"`
	Alternatives    []string `json:"alternatives,omitempty"`
	Notes           string   `json:"notes,omitempty"`
	Confidence      float64  `json:"confidence,omitempty"`
	Warnings        []string `json:"warnings,omitempty"`
	Truncated       bool     `json:"-"`
	Engine          string   `json:"engine,omitempty"`
	Model           string   `json:"model,omitempty"`
	Dictionary      *DictEntry     `json:"dictionary,omitempty"`
	Learn           *LearnResult   `json:"learn,omitempty"`
	Bilingual       map[int]string `json:"-"`
	Suggestions     []string `json:"suggestions,omitempty"`
	SuggestDistance int      `json:"-"`
}
```

Supporting payload structs (all in `engine.go`): `DictEntry` / `Meaning` / `Definition` (dict payload, JSON-tagged for API output), and `LearnResult` / `LearnIssue` / `LearnGloss` / `LearnExample` (tutor mode). These are already fully JSON-annotated — an HTTP handler can serialize `TranslateResult` directly.

### Streaming primitives (`engine.go:172-239`)

```go
type ChunkKind int
const ( ChunkToken ChunkKind = iota; ChunkDone; ChunkError )   // engine.go:175

type Chunk struct {            // engine.go:185
	Kind   ChunkKind
	Text   string            // token text (ChunkToken)
	Result *TranslateResult  // final result (ChunkDone)
	Err    error             // terminal error (ChunkError)
}

func Drain(ch <-chan Chunk, onToken func(string)) (*TranslateResult, error)  // engine.go:225
```

### Concrete engines — constructors and config structs

| Engine type | Constructor | Config struct | `Name()` | `Supports` |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `*LLMEngine` (`llm.go:31`) | `NewLLM(cfg LLMConfig) *LLMEngine` (`llm.go:38`) | `LLMConfig{Name,BaseURL,Model,APIKeyEnv,Timeout}` (`llm.go:22`) | provider name | `ModeTranslate` only |
| `*DictEngine` (`dict.go:32`) | `NewDict(cfg DictConfig) *DictEngine` (`dict.go:39`) | `DictConfig{Endpoint,Lang,Fuzzy,Wordlist,Timeout}` (`dict.go:22`) | `"dictionary"` | `ModeDict` only |
| `*LocalDictEngine` (`localdict.go:27`) | `NewLocalDict(cfg LocalDictConfig) *LocalDictEngine` (`localdict.go:35`) | `LocalDictConfig{Dir,CedictURL,EcdictURL,AutoDownload,Fuzzy,APIFallback,Timeout}` (`localdict.go:14`) | `"dictionary"` | `ModeDict` only |
| `*SmartDictEngine` (`smartdict.go:32`) | `NewSmartDict(dict, llm Engine, cfg SmartDictConfig) *SmartDictEngine` (`smartdict.go:39`) | `SmartDictConfig{CloseDistance,Preset}` (`smartdict.go:13`) | `"smart-dict"` | `ModeDict` only |
| `*SmartAutoEngine` (`smartauto.go:22`) | `NewSmartAuto(smart, llm Engine) *SmartAutoEngine` (`smartauto.go:29`) | — (composes two Engines) | `"smart-auto"` | `ModeTranslate` only |
| `*GoogleEngine` (`google.go:27`) | `NewGoogle(cfg GoogleConfig) *GoogleEngine` (`google.go:33`) | `GoogleConfig{Endpoint,ExtraDT,UserAgent,Timeout}` (`google.go:17`) | `"google"` | `ModeTranslate` only |
| `*Chain` (`chain.go:18`) | `NewChain(engines []Engine, ttl time.Duration) *Chain` (`chain.go:33`) | — | `"auto"` | true if any member supports it |

### Other exported helpers

- `func NormalizeModelID(id string) string` (`models.go:22`) — strips `[1m]`, dated suffixes.
- `type ModelRec struct{Role,ID,Note string}` + `var Recommended []ModelRec` (`models.go:32/41`).
- Preset constants: `PresetConcise`, `PresetContextual`, `PresetDictionary` (`prompt.go:12`).
- Dict data-file helpers: `DictDir`, `CedictPath`, `EcdictDBPath`, `DownloadCedict(ctx,url,dst,prog)`, `BuildEcdictDB(ctx,csvURL,dbPath,prog)` (`dictdata.go`).
- Sentinel errors (`errors.go`): `ErrEmptyInput`, `ErrNoResult`, `ErrNoEngineForMode`, `ErrAllEnginesFailed`, `ErrNoDictEntry`.

---

## 2. How translation is performed

There is no free function; translation is `Engine.Translate(ctx, req) (<-chan Chunk, error)`. **It is fully context-aware** — `ctx` is threaded into every HTTP request and every channel send (`select { case ch<-...: case <-ctx.Done(): }`).

**Uniform channel contract** (documented at `engine.go:192`): `Translate` returns a receive-only channel and MUST close it after exactly one terminal `Chunk` (`ChunkDone` or `ChunkError`). Streaming engines emit zero+ `ChunkToken` then one `ChunkDone`; non-streaming engines emit a single `ChunkDone`. The synchronous `error` return is **only** for immediate setup failures (e.g. `ErrEmptyInput`); all runtime/network errors flow as a terminal `ChunkError`. This lets the CLI's `Drain` loop and the TUI's channel reader share one path — and an HTTP/SSE handler would consume the same channel.

The main LLM implementation is `(*LLMEngine).Translate` (`llm.go:185`):
- Rejects empty input with `ErrEmptyInput` (unless a bilingual doc carries `Segments`).
- Resolves the model (`NormalizeModelID`, honoring a provider-scoped `req.Model` override).
- Dispatches on model id: Claude models → `translateAnthropic` (`/v1/messages`, `x-api-key`), everything else → `translateOpenAI` (`/chat/completions`, Bearer). Both are OpenAI-compatible base URLs (copilot-proxy, Ollama, OpenRouter, LiteLLM).
- Prompt selection via `promptFor(req)` (`llm.go:279`) → concise/contextual/dictionary translate prompt, or the learn/bilingual structured prompts (`prompt.go`).
- Result assembly in `finalizeResult` (`llm.go:292`): plain translate fills `Translation` + offline-detected `DetectedSource` (`lang.Detect`); learn/bilingual parse strict JSON defensively (`extractJSON`, falls back to raw text + a warning on parse failure). Truncated streams are flagged via `markTruncated` (adds `Truncated=true` + a warning) so partial output is shown but never cached as complete.

`Google` and the dict engines are non-streaming and return via the `single(res, err)` helper (`engine.go:211`), a closed 1-buffered channel.

---

## 3. "Define" / dictionary lookup — where sqlite comes in

**There is no separate `Define` method.** Definition lookup is `Translate` with `req.Mode == ModeDict`. The CLI's `define` command confirms this (`cmd/define.go:44`):

```go
ch, err := de.Translate(cmd.Context(), engine.Request{Text: word, Mode: engine.ModeDict, Stream: false})
res2, err := engine.Drain(ch, nil)
```

Three dict engines serve `ModeDict`:

1. **`DictEngine`** (`dict.go`) — online, hits the Free Dictionary API (`dictionaryapi.dev`), exact match then a local fuzzy fallback on 404 using `/usr/share/dict/words` + Levenshtein (`nearestN`). Returns `Suggestions`/`SuggestDistance` on a miss.

2. **`LocalDictEngine`** (`localdict.go`) — offline bilingual, routed by script (`lang.IsChinese`): **Chinese → CC-CEDICT**, **English → ECDICT**. This is where **sqlite** enters:
   - **ECDICT (en→zh)** is a **SQLite database** queried on disk via `modernc.org/sqlite` (pure-Go driver, no cgo). `ecdictDB` (`ecdict.go:17`) opens lazily read-only (`?_pragma=query_only(true)`) and does point lookups: `SELECT ... FROM entries WHERE word_lc = ? LIMIT 1` (`ecdict.go:52`). DB path: `EcdictDBPath(dir)` = `<dict dir>/ecdict.db` (`dictdata.go:31`). The DB is built by `BuildEcdictDB` (`dictdata.go:84`) from a ~63 MB CSV into an indexed SQLite (schema at `dictdata.go:33`; `CREATE INDEX idx_word_lc`).
   - **CC-CEDICT (zh→en)** is NOT sqlite — it's an in-memory index (`cedictIndex`, `cedict.go:22`) parsed from the text file `cedict_ts.u8`, keyed on both traditional and simplified forms, with prefix-based suggestions.

3. **`SmartDictEngine`** (`smartdict.go`) — composes a dict engine + an LLM: answers from the dictionary on an exact hit or a close-enough typo (`SuggestDistance <= CloseDistance`), otherwise **falls back to the LLM** in translate mode (with a `PresetDictionary` prompt and a stamped `Warning` so the downgrade is never silent).

`SmartAutoEngine` (`smartauto.go`) is the "smart default": `isLookup()` routes a single word/short CJK term to the smart-dict path and a phrase/sentence to the LLM — all still under one `ModeTranslate` `Translate` call.

---

## 4. Streaming at the engine level

Streaming is **channel-based**, not `io.Writer`/callback at the engine boundary. The engine returns `<-chan Chunk`; tokens arrive as `ChunkToken{Text}`, then one terminal `ChunkDone{Result}` or `ChunkError{Err}`. The callback (`onToken func(string)`) exists only in the consumer helper `Drain` (`engine.go:225`), not in the interface.

- Streaming is **LLM-only** and gated by `req.Stream`. `translateOpenAI`/`translateAnthropic` set `stream := req.Stream && !req.Learn && !req.Bilingual` (structured JSON modes always run non-streaming, `llm.go:419` / `llm.go:494`).
- SSE parsing: `readSSE` (OpenAI `data:` deltas + `[DONE]`, `llm.go:583`) and `readAnthropicSSE` (Anthropic `content_block_delta`/`message_stop`, `llm.go:628`). Both accumulate a `strings.Builder`, emit a `ChunkToken` per delta, detect truncation (`finish_reason=="length"` / `stop_reason=="max_tokens"` / stream closed with no terminal marker), and honor `ctx` on every send.
- Channels are buffered (`make(chan Chunk, 32)`) so a fast stream doesn't block on slow renders.
- Non-streaming engines (google, all dict engines) ignore `req.Stream` and emit a single `ChunkDone` via `single()`.

For `translate serve`: an SSE handler maps `ChunkToken → data: <text>` and `ChunkDone/ChunkError → terminal event`. Note the caveat recorded in the backlog doc (`backlog/api-server.md:26`): copilot-proxy still buffers Claude `/v1/messages`, so visible streaming stays limited there regardless of transport.

---

## 5. How the engine is constructed — dependencies

Each engine has a small `NewXxx(cfg)` constructor taking a plain config struct (shown in §1). **The full stack is assembled outside the package**, in `cmd/build.go` (the wiring an HTTP adapter would replicate), driven by `config.Resolved`:

- `buildEngine(res config.Resolved) (engine.Engine, error)` (`cmd/build.go:16`) switches on `res.Engine`: `"auto"` → `buildAutoChain`; `"smartauto"` → `smartAutoFromConfig`; `"google"`; `"llm"`/named provider → `llmFromProvider`.
- `llmFromProvider` (`cmd/build.go:72`) → `engine.NewLLM(LLMConfig{Name,BaseURL,Model,APIKeyEnv})`. **The API key is resolved once at construction** from `os.Getenv(cfg.APIKeyEnv)` inside `NewLLM` (`llm.go:42`) — not per request.
- `dictFromConfig` (`cmd/build.go:96`) builds either the API `DictEngine` or the offline `LocalDictEngine` (with dict `Dir`, `CedictURL`, `EcdictURL`, `AutoDownload`, optional API fallback).
- `buildAutoChain` (`cmd/build.go:49`) assembles `NewChain(engines, 5*time.Second)` from `cfg.Chain.Order`.

**Dependencies an engine needs**: an OpenAI-compatible base URL + model + API-key env name (LLM); an HTTP client (created internally with a timeout); dict data paths / wordlist (dict engines); and for smart engines, references to other `Engine` values. 

**Crucially, the engine needs neither a config object at call time nor a history store.** `config.Resolved`/`config.Config` are consumed only by the `cmd` builders, and `internal/store` is never imported by `engine`. The `HTTP client`, `*sql.DB`, and dict indexes are the only long-lived resources; there is no `Close()` on the `Engine` interface (the sqlite DB is opened lazily and left open for the process lifetime).

---

## 6. Concurrency — is the engine goroutine-safe?

**Broadly yes — safe to call `Translate` concurrently from many goroutines** (exactly what an HTTP server needs), with these specifics:

- **Stateless engines** (`LLMEngine`, `GoogleEngine`, `DictEngine`): after construction their fields (`cfg`, resolved `key`, `*http.Client`) are **immutable**; `*http.Client` is safe for concurrent use; each `Translate` spins its own goroutine + channel. No shared mutable state. Fully concurrency-safe.
- **`Chain`** (`chain.go:18`) — the one engine holding **mutable state**: an availability cache `avail map[string]probeCache`, but it is guarded by a `sync.Mutex` (`recentlyDown`/`mark`, `chain.go:56-67`). Safe for concurrent use.
- **Lazy dict loaders** hold mutable state gated by `sync.Once`: `wordIndex` (`dict.go:206`), `cedictIndex` (`cedict.go:22`), `ecdictDB` (`ecdict.go:17`). `sync.Once` makes first-use loading race-free; after load the maps/DB handle are read-only. `*sql.DB` is itself concurrency-safe (connection pool). Safe.
- **Composed engines** (`SmartAuto`, `SmartDict`) only hold immutable `Engine` references + config. Safe.

No engine mutates `Request` in place destructively for the caller — routing engines copy (`r := req`) before changing `Mode`/`Target` (`smartauto.go:55`, `smartdict.go:109`). The returned `*TranslateResult` is freshly allocated per call.

The one **cross-process** concurrency concern is not in the engine at all: the backlog doc (`backlog/api-server.md:53`) flags that server + CLI both appending `history.jsonl` requires a concurrency-safe store (append + lock) — that's the `store` layer (§7), not `engine`.

---

## 7. What the adapter must supply itself (the glue outside the engine)

Because the engine is deliberately TUI-free and store-free, an HTTP handler must replicate the small glue that `cmd/root.go` performs today:

- **Request routing / target selection**: pair-mode target is computed at the call site via `lang.PairTarget(home, away, text)` in `effectiveTarget` (`cmd/root.go:268`); the `Request` is then built literally (`cmd/root.go:469`).
- **Consuming the stream**: `engine.Drain(ch, onTok)` (`cmd/root.go:503`) — for SSE, replace `onTok` with an SSE flush.
- **History persistence**: handled after the fact by `recordAndRemember(...)` (`cmd/root.go:256`), writing through `internal/store`. The store interface is `store.Store` (`internal/store/store.go:27`: `Add`/`Recent`/`Search`/`Close`), backed today by an append-only JSONL (`store.OpenJSONL(path)`, `store/jsonl.go:25`). The `TranslateResult`/`store.Record` shapes are aligned but distinct — the adapter maps one to the other. This is the store the backlog doc says must become append-locked for concurrent server+CLI writes.

**Net for `translate serve`**: the engine is a clean, context-aware, concurrency-safe core you can wrap directly (`Engine.Translate` → JSON for `POST /v1/translate` and `POST /v1/define` via `Mode`, and → SSE for `GET /v1/translate/stream` by ranging the channel). The two things it does not give you and you must add at the HTTP layer are (a) pair-target routing (`lang.PairTarget`) and (b) history read/write (`internal/store`, made concurrency-safe).

# `internal/engine` — deep dive for the `translate serve` HTTP adapter

## Package shape (no sub-packages)

`internal/engine` is a **single flat Go package** — there are no sub-packages under it. All 12 non-test source files share `package engine`:

`engine.go` (core types/interface), `chain.go`, `llm.go`, `dict.go`, `localdict.go`, `cedict.go`, `ecdict.go`, `dictdata.go`, `google.go`, `smartauto.go`, `smartdict.go`, `models.go`, `prompt.go`, `errors.go`. The package imports zero TUI code (it depends only on `internal/lang`, `internal/debug`, `internal/xdgpath`, and two third-party libs: `modernc.org/sqlite`, `github.com/agnivade/levenshtein`). The engine layer does **not** import `internal/store` — history persistence lives entirely at the call site (see §7).

---

## 1. Public API surface

### The central abstraction — `Engine` interface (`internal/engine/engine.go:201`)

```go
type Engine interface {
	Name() string
	Translate(ctx context.Context, req Request) (&lt;-chan Chunk, error)
	Detect(ctx context.Context, text string) (string, error)
	Available(ctx context.Context) bool
	Supports(m Mode) bool
}
```

Every method takes/honors `context.Context`. There is a **single entry point for all work — `Translate`** — parameterized by `Request.Mode` (translate vs dict). There is no separate `Define`/`Detect`-heavy surface (see §3).

Two secondary interfaces:
- `ModelLister` (`llm.go:203`): `Models(ctx context.Context) ([]string, error)` — implemented by `*LLMEngine`.

### Modes and request/result types (`engine.go`)

```go
type Mode int
const ( ModeTranslate Mode = iota; ModeDict )   // engine.go:14

type Request struct {          // engine.go:23
	Text          string
	Source        string  // BCP-47-ish or "auto"/""
	Target        string  // "en", "zh", ...
	Mode          Mode
	MaxAlts       int
	Stream        bool    // caller wants token streaming (LLM only)
	Model         string  // per-request model override
	ModelProvider string  // scopes Model to one provider
	Preset        string  // "" =&gt; concise
	Extra         string  // extra user instructions appended to system prompt
	Pair          bool    // bidirectional pair mode
	PairHome      string
	PairAway      string
	Learn         bool    // structured tutor mode (always non-streaming)
	Bilingual     bool    // whole-doc mode (always non-streaming)
	Segments      []Segment
}

type Segment struct { Text string; Code bool }   // engine.go:65
```

```go
type TranslateResult struct {   // engine.go:72 — also the history-record shape
	Translation     string   `json:"translation"`
	DetectedSource  string   `json:"detected_source,omitempty"`
	Target          string   `json:"target"`
	Alternatives    []string `json:"alternatives,omitempty"`
	Notes           string   `json:"notes,omitempty"`
	Confidence      float64  `json:"confidence,omitempty"`
	Warnings        []string `json:"warnings,omitempty"`
	Truncated       bool     `json:"-"`
	Engine          string   `json:"engine,omitempty"`
	Model           string   `json:"model,omitempty"`
	Dictionary      *DictEntry     `json:"dictionary,omitempty"`
	Learn           *LearnResult   `json:"learn,omitempty"`
	Bilingual       map[int]string `json:"-"`
	Suggestions     []string `json:"suggestions,omitempty"`
	SuggestDistance int      `json:"-"`
}
```

Supporting payload structs (all in `engine.go`): `DictEntry` / `Meaning` / `Definition` (dict payload, JSON-tagged for API output), and `LearnResult` / `LearnIssue` / `LearnGloss` / `LearnExample` (tutor mode). These are already fully JSON-annotated — an HTTP handler can serialize `TranslateResult` directly.

### Streaming primitives (`engine.go:172-239`)

```go
type ChunkKind int
const ( ChunkToken ChunkKind = iota; ChunkDone; ChunkError )   // engine.go:175

type Chunk struct {            // engine.go:185
	Kind   ChunkKind
	Text   string            // token text (ChunkToken)
	Result *TranslateResult  // final result (ChunkDone)
	Err    error             // terminal error (ChunkError)
}

func Drain(ch &lt;-chan Chunk, onToken func(string)) (*TranslateResult, error)  // engine.go:225
```

### Concrete engines — constructors and config structs

| Engine type | Constructor | Config struct | `Name()` | `Supports` |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `*LLMEngine` (`llm.go:31`) | `NewLLM(cfg LLMConfig) *LLMEngine` (`llm.go:38`) | `LLMConfig{Name,BaseURL,Model,APIKeyEnv,Timeout}` (`llm.go:22`) | provider name | `ModeTranslate` only |
| `*DictEngine` (`dict.go:32`) | `NewDict(cfg DictConfig) *DictEngine` (`dict.go:39`) | `DictConfig{Endpoint,Lang,Fuzzy,Wordlist,Timeout}` (`dict.go:22`) | `"dictionary"` | `ModeDict` only |
| `*LocalDictEngine` (`localdict.go:27`) | `NewLocalDict(cfg LocalDictConfig) *LocalDictEngine` (`localdict.go:35`) | `LocalDictConfig{Dir,CedictURL,EcdictURL,AutoDownload,Fuzzy,APIFallback,Timeout}` (`localdict.go:14`) | `"dictionary"` | `ModeDict` only |
| `*SmartDictEngine` (`smartdict.go:32`) | `NewSmartDict(dict, llm Engine, cfg SmartDictConfig) *SmartDictEngine` (`smartdict.go:39`) | `SmartDictConfig{CloseDistance,Preset}` (`smartdict.go:13`) | `"smart-dict"` | `ModeDict` only |
| `*SmartAutoEngine` (`smartauto.go:22`) | `NewSmartAuto(smart, llm Engine) *SmartAutoEngine` (`smartauto.go:29`) | — (composes two Engines) | `"smart-auto"` | `ModeTranslate` only |
| `*GoogleEngine` (`google.go:27`) | `NewGoogle(cfg GoogleConfig) *GoogleEngine` (`google.go:33`) | `GoogleConfig{Endpoint,ExtraDT,UserAgent,Timeout}` (`google.go:17`) | `"google"` | `ModeTranslate` only |
| `*Chain` (`chain.go:18`) | `NewChain(engines []Engine, ttl time.Duration) *Chain` (`chain.go:33`) | — | `"auto"` | true if any member supports it |

### Other exported helpers

- `func NormalizeModelID(id string) string` (`models.go:22`) — strips `[1m]`, dated suffixes.
- `type ModelRec struct{Role,ID,Note string}` + `var Recommended []ModelRec` (`models.go:32/41`).
- Preset constants: `PresetConcise`, `PresetContextual`, `PresetDictionary` (`prompt.go:12`).
- Dict data-file helpers: `DictDir`, `CedictPath`, `EcdictDBPath`, `DownloadCedict(ctx,url,dst,prog)`, `BuildEcdictDB(ctx,csvURL,dbPath,prog)` (`dictdata.go`).
- Sentinel errors (`errors.go`): `ErrEmptyInput`, `ErrNoResult`, `ErrNoEngineForMode`, `ErrAllEnginesFailed`, `ErrNoDictEntry`.

---

## 2. How translation is performed

There is no free function; translation is `Engine.Translate(ctx, req) (&lt;-chan Chunk, error)`. **It is fully context-aware** — `ctx` is threaded into every HTTP request and every channel send (`select { case ch&lt;-...: case &lt;-ctx.Done(): }`).

**Uniform channel contract** (documented at `engine.go:192`): `Translate` returns a receive-only channel and MUST close it after exactly one terminal `Chunk` (`ChunkDone` or `ChunkError`). Streaming engines emit zero+ `ChunkToken` then one `ChunkDone`; non-streaming engines emit a single `ChunkDone`. The synchronous `error` return is **only** for immediate setup failures (e.g. `ErrEmptyInput`); all runtime/network errors flow as a terminal `ChunkError`. This lets the CLI's `Drain` loop and the TUI's channel reader share one path — and an HTTP/SSE handler would consume the same channel.

The main LLM implementation is `(*LLMEngine).Translate` (`llm.go:185`):
- Rejects empty input with `ErrEmptyInput` (unless a bilingual doc carries `Segments`).
- Resolves the model (`NormalizeModelID`, honoring a provider-scoped `req.Model` override).
- Dispatches on model id: Claude models → `translateAnthropic` (`/v1/messages`, `x-api-key`), everything else → `translateOpenAI` (`/chat/completions`, Bearer). Both are OpenAI-compatible base URLs (copilot-proxy, Ollama, OpenRouter, LiteLLM).
- Prompt selection via `promptFor(req)` (`llm.go:279`) → concise/contextual/dictionary translate prompt, or the learn/bilingual structured prompts (`prompt.go`).
- Result assembly in `finalizeResult` (`llm.go:292`): plain translate fills `Translation` + offline-detected `DetectedSource` (`lang.Detect`); learn/bilingual parse strict JSON defensively (`extractJSON`, falls back to raw text + a warning on parse failure). Truncated streams are flagged via `markTruncated` (adds `Truncated=true` + a warning) so partial output is shown but never cached as complete.

`Google` and the dict engines are non-streaming and return via the `single(res, err)` helper (`engine.go:211`), a closed 1-buffered channel.

---

## 3. "Define" / dictionary lookup — where sqlite comes in

**There is no separate `Define` method.** Definition lookup is `Translate` with `req.Mode == ModeDict`. The CLI's `define` command confirms this (`cmd/define.go:44`):

```go
ch, err := de.Translate(cmd.Context(), engine.Request{Text: word, Mode: engine.ModeDict, Stream: false})
res2, err := engine.Drain(ch, nil)
```

Three dict engines serve `ModeDict`:

1. **`DictEngine`** (`dict.go`) — online, hits the Free Dictionary API (`dictionaryapi.dev`), exact match then a local fuzzy fallback on 404 using `/usr/share/dict/words` + Levenshtein (`nearestN`). Returns `Suggestions`/`SuggestDistance` on a miss.

2. **`LocalDictEngine`** (`localdict.go`) — offline bilingual, routed by script (`lang.IsChinese`): **Chinese → CC-CEDICT**, **English → ECDICT**. This is where **sqlite** enters:
   - **ECDICT (en→zh)** is a **SQLite database** queried on disk via `modernc.org/sqlite` (pure-Go driver, no cgo). `ecdictDB` (`ecdict.go:17`) opens lazily read-only (`?_pragma=query_only(true)`) and does point lookups: `SELECT ... FROM entries WHERE word_lc = ? LIMIT 1` (`ecdict.go:52`). DB path: `EcdictDBPath(dir)` = `&lt;dict dir&gt;/ecdict.db` (`dictdata.go:31`). The DB is built by `BuildEcdictDB` (`dictdata.go:84`) from a ~63 MB CSV into an indexed SQLite (schema at `dictdata.go:33`; `CREATE INDEX idx_word_lc`).
   - **CC-CEDICT (zh→en)** is NOT sqlite — it's an in-memory index (`cedictIndex`, `cedict.go:22`) parsed from the text file `cedict_ts.u8`, keyed on both traditional and simplified forms, with prefix-based suggestions.

3. **`SmartDictEngine`** (`smartdict.go`) — composes a dict engine + an LLM: answers from the dictionary on an exact hit or a close-enough typo (`SuggestDistance &lt;= CloseDistance`), otherwise **falls back to the LLM** in translate mode (with a `PresetDictionary` prompt and a stamped `Warning` so the downgrade is never silent).

`SmartAutoEngine` (`smartauto.go`) is the "smart default": `isLookup()` routes a single word/short CJK term to the smart-dict path and a phrase/sentence to the LLM — all still under one `ModeTranslate` `Translate` call.

---

## 4. Streaming at the engine level

Streaming is **channel-based**, not `io.Writer`/callback at the engine boundary. The engine returns `&lt;-chan Chunk`; tokens arrive as `ChunkToken{Text}`, then one terminal `ChunkDone{Result}` or `ChunkError{Err}`. The callback (`onToken func(string)`) exists only in the consumer helper `Drain` (`engine.go:225`), not in the interface.

- Streaming is **LLM-only** and gated by `req.Stream`. `translateOpenAI`/`translateAnthropic` set `stream := req.Stream &amp;&amp; !req.Learn &amp;&amp; !req.Bilingual` (structured JSON modes always run non-streaming, `llm.go:419` / `llm.go:494`).
- SSE parsing: `readSSE` (OpenAI `data:` deltas + `[DONE]`, `llm.go:583`) and `readAnthropicSSE` (Anthropic `content_block_delta`/`message_stop`, `llm.go:628`). Both accumulate a `strings.Builder`, emit a `ChunkToken` per delta, detect truncation (`finish_reason=="length"` / `stop_reason=="max_tokens"` / stream closed with no terminal marker), and honor `ctx` on every send.
- Channels are buffered (`make(chan Chunk, 32)`) so a fast stream doesn't block on slow renders.
- Non-streaming engines (google, all dict engines) ignore `req.Stream` and emit a single `ChunkDone` via `single()`.

For `translate serve`: an SSE handler maps `ChunkToken → data: &lt;text&gt;` and `ChunkDone/ChunkError → terminal event`. Note the caveat recorded in the backlog doc (`backlog/api-server.md:26`): copilot-proxy still buffers Claude `/v1/messages`, so visible streaming stays limited there regardless of transport.

---

## 5. How the engine is constructed — dependencies

Each engine has a small `NewXxx(cfg)` constructor taking a plain config struct (shown in §1). **The full stack is assembled outside the package**, in `cmd/build.go` (the wiring an HTTP adapter would replicate), driven by `config.Resolved`:

- `buildEngine(res config.Resolved) (engine.Engine, error)` (`cmd/build.go:16`) switches on `res.Engine`: `"auto"` → `buildAutoChain`; `"smartauto"` → `smartAutoFromConfig`; `"google"`; `"llm"`/named provider → `llmFromProvider`.
- `llmFromProvider` (`cmd/build.go:72`) → `engine.NewLLM(LLMConfig{Name,BaseURL,Model,APIKeyEnv})`. **The API key is resolved once at construction** from `os.Getenv(cfg.APIKeyEnv)` inside `NewLLM` (`llm.go:42`) — not per request.
- `dictFromConfig` (`cmd/build.go:96`) builds either the API `DictEngine` or the offline `LocalDictEngine` (with dict `Dir`, `CedictURL`, `EcdictURL`, `AutoDownload`, optional API fallback).
- `buildAutoChain` (`cmd/build.go:49`) assembles `NewChain(engines, 5*time.Second)` from `cfg.Chain.Order`.

**Dependencies an engine needs**: an OpenAI-compatible base URL + model + API-key env name (LLM); an HTTP client (created internally with a timeout); dict data paths / wordlist (dict engines); and for smart engines, references to other `Engine` values. 

**Crucially, the engine needs neither a config object at call time nor a history store.** `config.Resolved`/`config.Config` are consumed only by the `cmd` builders, and `internal/store` is never imported by `engine`. The `HTTP client`, `*sql.DB`, and dict indexes are the only long-lived resources; there is no `Close()` on the `Engine` interface (the sqlite DB is opened lazily and left open for the process lifetime).

---

## 6. Concurrency — is the engine goroutine-safe?

**Broadly yes — safe to call `Translate` concurrently from many goroutines** (exactly what an HTTP server needs), with these specifics:

- **Stateless engines** (`LLMEngine`, `GoogleEngine`, `DictEngine`): after construction their fields (`cfg`, resolved `key`, `*http.Client`) are **immutable**; `*http.Client` is safe for concurrent use; each `Translate` spins its own goroutine + channel. No shared mutable state. Fully concurrency-safe.
- **`Chain`** (`chain.go:18`) — the one engine holding **mutable state**: an availability cache `avail map[string]probeCache`, but it is guarded by a `sync.Mutex` (`recentlyDown`/`mark`, `chain.go:56-67`). Safe for concurrent use.
- **Lazy dict loaders** hold mutable state gated by `sync.Once`: `wordIndex` (`dict.go:206`), `cedictIndex` (`cedict.go:22`), `ecdictDB` (`ecdict.go:17`). `sync.Once` makes first-use loading race-free; after load the maps/DB handle are read-only. `*sql.DB` is itself concurrency-safe (connection pool). Safe.
- **Composed engines** (`SmartAuto`, `SmartDict`) only hold immutable `Engine` references + config. Safe.

No engine mutates `Request` in place destructively for the caller — routing engines copy (`r := req`) before changing `Mode`/`Target` (`smartauto.go:55`, `smartdict.go:109`). The returned `*TranslateResult` is freshly allocated per call.

The one **cross-process** concurrency concern is not in the engine at all: the backlog doc (`backlog/api-server.md:53`) flags that server + CLI both appending `history.jsonl` requires a concurrency-safe store (append + lock) — that's the `store` layer (§7), not `engine`.

---

## 7. What the adapter must supply itself (the glue outside the engine)

Because the engine is deliberately TUI-free and store-free, an HTTP handler must replicate the small glue that `cmd/root.go` performs today:

- **Request routing / target selection**: pair-mode target is computed at the call site via `lang.PairTarget(home, away, text)` in `effectiveTarget` (`cmd/root.go:268`); the `Request` is then built literally (`cmd/root.go:469`).
- **Consuming the stream**: `engine.Drain(ch, onTok)` (`cmd/root.go:503`) — for SSE, replace `onTok` with an SSE flush.
- **History persistence**: handled after the fact by `recordAndRemember(...)` (`cmd/root.go:256`), writing through `internal/store`. The store interface is `store.Store` (`internal/store/store.go:27`: `Add`/`Recent`/`Search`/`Close`), backed today by an append-only JSONL (`store.OpenJSONL(path)`, `store/jsonl.go:25`). The `TranslateResult`/`store.Record` shapes are aligned but distinct — the adapter maps one to the other. This is the store the backlog doc says must become append-locked for concurrent server+CLI writes.

**Net for `translate serve`**: the engine is a clean, context-aware, concurrency-safe core you can wrap directly (`Engine.Translate` → JSON for `POST /v1/translate` and `POST /v1/define` via `Mode`, and → SSE for `GET /v1/translate/stream` by ranging the channel). The two things it does not give you and you must add at the HTTP layer are (a) pair-target routing (`lang.PairTarget`) and (b) history read/write (`internal/store`, made concurrency-safe).</result>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
I've got a clear picture now: the translate module is built in Go 1.26.4 with cobra handling CLI commands through factories in the root file, while the core engine lives in a single flat package that's TUI and store agnostic, context-aware, and thread-safe, with the main translation method streaming results back as chunks.

The builders for engines, LLMs, dictionaries, and chains are currently in the `cmd` package rather than `internal/engine`, which creates a layering problem if I want to add a new `internal/server` package—I'd either need to move the server into `cmd` or lift those builders to a shared layer to avoid import cycles. Configuration is TOML-based with `config.Load()` and `cfg.Resolve()` for overrides, and there's no `[server]` section yet. The store is a simple interface backed by a jsonl implementation with in-process mutex locking, and history gets persisted at the call site through `recordAndRemember`. Language pair routing happens via `lang.PairTarget` in the effective target logic, and SSE is already handled by the engine parsing upstream streams while a handler ranges over the channel.

The user is asking me to design an API server with Swagger/OpenAPI and consider whether to also include MCP server functionality—this is a planning task, not an implementation request. The key question is whether MCP should be folded into the same server or kept separate, which requires thinking through how MCP (Model Context Protocol) works as a way for LLM clients to interact with tools.

MCP can run over stdio for local use or over Streamable HTTP for network access. Since we're already building an HTTP server, we could potentially run MCP over the same HTTP server and port, or keep it as a separate stdio-based subcommand. The main tension is that adding an MCP dependency conflicts with the current "stdlib only" philosophy, so I need to weigh whether that trade-off makes sense for the design.

The cleaner approach is to extract a service layer in `internal/service` that both the HTTP API and MCP adapters can call. This service would hold the engine, store, and config, and expose methods like `Translate` that handle the core logic — pair-target routing, history management, and engine building — so that logic isn't duplicated across adapters. specific stuff), and `cmd/build.go` becomes a thin re-export or the cmd commands call the service builders.

I'm weighing whether to move the builders out of `cmd` into a shared service package for cleaner architecture supporting both HTTP and MCP, or keep things minimal by putting the HTTP server directly in `cmd` and reusing `buildEngine` there without additional refactoring.

The clean approach would be creating `internal/apiserver` and `internal/mcpserver` packages that both depend on a new `internal/service` core owning engine building and store logic, which means lifting `buildEngine` from `cmd/build.go` into the service layer so both the HTTP and MCP servers can use it without duplication. Since the user asked for design rather than just minimal implementation, I should recommend the clean architecture as the primary approach while acknowledging the simpler fallback.

For MCP specifically, I'm thinking it should be included as a separate subcommand `translate mcp` using stdio transport—the standard for local MCP clients like Claude Desktop—which lets it share the same service core without bloating the HTTP server. For the MCP implementation, I need to weigh using the official Go SDK against the project's minimal-dependency philosophy—hand-rolling JSON-RPC 2.0 over stdio is feasible for a few tools but the official SDK handles protocol negotiation and capabilities more robustly, so I'll recommend it while flagging the trade-off. I should do some targeted web searches to verify the current state of the MCP Go SDK landscape and the best transport approach, then move into the actual design work. Given the scope of this task, I'm thinking I should launch a Plan agent in Phase 2 to explore multiple design approaches systematically.

But I need to be careful not to over-orchestrate with Workflow tools when the plan-mode structure already gives me clear phases to follow. I'll stick with the plan-mode workflow as my primary guide while enriching it with web research on the side. So my next steps are: research the MCP Go SDK maturity and transport options (stdio vs HTTP), look into serving OpenAPI/Swagger UI from stdlib, then hand off the architecture design to a Plan agent with full context. I'll spin up the Plan agent to map out the architecture—the shared service layer, HTTP and MCP server implementations, package structure, config schema, and phasing strategy—while I do a quick web search on the current Go MCP SDK options and confirm the OpenAPI/Swagger UI embedding approach.

_**Agent - sidechain (2026-07-22 10:06:54Z)**_

Design a detailed implementation architecture for adding a local HTTP API server (`translate serve`) with OpenAPI/Swagger, AND an MCP (Model Context Protocol) server, to an existing Go CLI translation tool at /Users/david/src/tries/2026-07-09-translate-cli-tui. This is a DESIGN task — output a step-by-step implementation plan, not code. Read the actual files to ground your design.

## Established facts (from prior exploration — do not re-verify from scratch, but read files to design against them)

- Module `github.com/daviddwlee84/translate`, Go 1.26.4. CLI uses cobra; subcommands registered in `cmd/root.go` (~line 95) via `newXxxCmd()` factory pattern. Add `serve` and `mcp` there. Persistent flags incl. `--json`/`--stream` are package `cmd` vars.
- Core: `internal/engine` is a flat, TUI-free, store-free, context-aware, concurrency-safe package. Single entry point: `Engine.Translate(ctx, engine.Request) (<-chan engine.Chunk, error)`; `Request.Mode` is `ModeTranslate` or `ModeDict` (define = ModeDict). `TranslateResult` (internal/engine/engine.go:72) is fully JSON-tagged and is the ready HTTP/JSON response body. Streaming is channel-based: `ChunkToken`, then terminal `ChunkDone{Result}` / `ChunkError{Err}`. Consumer helper `engine.Drain(ch, onToken)`.
- CRITICAL LAYERING PROBLEM: engine builders live in package `cmd`, not `internal/engine`. Files: `cmd/build.go` has `buildEngine(res config.Resolved) (engine.Engine, error)`, `llmFromProvider`, `dictFromConfig`, `buildAutoChain`. A new `internal/server` package cannot import package `cmd`. Design must resolve this — evaluate: (a) put server code in package `cmd`, vs (b) lift the builders into a new shared package (e.g. `internal/appcore` or `internal/service`) that both `cmd` and the servers import. Recommend one, with the concrete refactor steps and which existing call sites change.
- Config: TOML via pelletier/go-toml/v2. `config.Load()` then `cfg.Resolve(overrides, config.ModeCLI)` → `config.Resolved`. No `[server]` table exists. SchemaVersion currently 1 (internal/config/config.go). Adding a `[server]` table is additive; the auto-upgrade path is in cmd/root.go (~lines 146-158). Design the `[server]` struct (port default e.g. 4155, bind loopback, optional token) and Default() changes.
- History store: `internal/store` — interface `store.Store{ Add, Recent, Search, Close }`, jsonl backend (`store/jsonl.go`) with an in-process `sync.Mutex` only — NO cross-process file lock (no flock). `store.Record` and `engine.TranslateResult` are distinct shapes; call site maps between them (`recordAndRemember` in cmd/root.go). Design how the long-lived server shares one Store across handler goroutines (mutex covers in-process), and address the server+CLI concurrent-append concern (evaluate adding an flock / O_APPEND-atomic-write note).
- Pair-target routing glue: `lang.PairTarget(home, away, text)` used in `effectiveTarget` (cmd/root.go:268). The adapter must replicate target selection.
- SSE: the LLM engine already parses upstream SSE and emits Chunks; an SSE handler just ranges the channel and flushes. CAVEAT: copilot-proxy buffers Claude `/v1/messages`, so visible token streaming stays limited there regardless of transport — this is upstream, transport-independent. Factor this into any streaming claims.
- MCP: 100% greenfield — no MCP code/deps exist. Deps are stdlib-only; no HTTP framework, no OpenAPI generator, no JSON-schema lib. Backlog wants stdlib net/http + hand-written /openapi.json + go:embed Swagger UI.

## Backlog spec to honor (backlog/api-server.md — READ IT)
Endpoints proposed: `POST /v1/translate`, `POST /v1/define`, `GET /v1/history[?q=]`, SSE `GET /v1/translate/stream`. Loopback-only bind mandatory (history is personal data). Optional token for /v1/history. Extension integration is OPT-IN with spawn fallback (don't break non-server users). Option C (translate raycast dev) is rejected — ignore it.

## What to design and output

1. **Package layout & layering fix**: exact new packages/files, and the refactor to resolve the cmd-builder import problem. Show the dependency direction. Recommend ONE approach decisively.

2. **Shared service core**: design a `Service` type (methods for Translate, TranslateStream, Define, History-recent, History-search) that both the HTTP server and the MCP server call, wrapping engine build + store + pair-target glue. Give method signatures. It should build the engine ONCE (warm) at startup and hold the Store.

3. **HTTP server (`translate serve`)**: cobra command + flags (--port, --bind, --token, --no-history?); handler for each endpoint; SSE handler design (ranging the channel, http.Flusher, r.Context() cancellation on client disconnect, preserving the Truncated warning); request/response JSON shapes (reuse TranslateResult); error model (JSON error envelope + status codes); graceful shutdown (signal + http.Server.Shutdown). 

4. **OpenAPI + Swagger UI**: decide hand-written /openapi.json vs a generator (swaggo/huma) — recommend one given the zero-framework philosophy; how to serve Swagger UI via go:embed (which assets, or a pinned CDN-less bundle); route (e.g. /docs, /openapi.json).

5. **MCP server (`translate mcp`)**: RECOMMEND whether to fold MCP in and HOW. Evaluate stdio transport (for Claude Desktop/Code/Cursor spawning the binary) vs Streamable-HTTP transport (sharing the serve port). Evaluate official Go SDK `github.com/modelcontextprotocol/go-sdk` vs `github.com/mark3labs/mcp-go` vs hand-rolling JSON-RPC-2.0-over-stdio for ~3 tools (translate, define, history). Give the tool schemas (tool name, input schema, what it returns). Address the dependency trade-off vs the project's minimal-dep philosophy. Decide: separate `translate mcp` subcommand (stdio) vs `/mcp` route on the serve HTTP server — or both.

6. **Config schema**: the `[server]` table fields + defaults + SchemaVersion handling.

7. **Security**: loopback bind enforcement, optional bearer token (which endpoints), and why MCP-stdio sidesteps all of this.

8. **Incremental delivery / phasing**: order the work into shippable phases (e.g. Phase 1: service core refactor + REST JSON; Phase 2: SSE; Phase 3: OpenAPI+Swagger; Phase 4: MCP). Note what can ship independently.

9. **Testing strategy**: httptest for handlers, table tests for the service, how to test SSE, how to smoke-test MCP (JSON-RPC handshake). Reference existing test patterns (just test globs ./cmd/... and ./internal/...).

10. **Risks / open questions** to surface to the user.

Be concrete: name files, function signatures, and the specific existing functions to reuse (with file:line). Keep the plan executable by another engineer. Prefer reusing existing code over new code.

I ran the web search you requested. Below are the highest‑relevance results and a short summary (stdio vs. streamable HTTP) with source citations — tell me which item(s) you want opened or explored in more detail.

Top matches
- modelcontextprotocol/go-sdk (GitHub) — official Go SDK repo (source code, examples, issues, recent commits). ([github.com](https://github.com/modelcontextprotocol/go-sdk))  
- MCP Go SDK docs (go.sdk.modelcontextprotocol.io) — SDK documentation pages (Transports, Lifecycle, Stdio and Streamable Transport docs). ([go.sdk.modelcontextprotocol.io](https://go.sdk.modelcontextprotocol.io/))  
- MCP specification — Transports section (defines stdio and streamable HTTP, including recommendations). ([modelcontextprotocol.io](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-06-18/basic/transports))  
- streamable.go in the go-sdk repo — SDK implementation file for the streamable transport (source code). ([github.com](https://github.com/modelcontextprotocol/go-sdk/blob/main/mcp/streamable.go))  
- HTTP example in the go-sdk examples — demonstrates using the streamable transport over HTTP (server + client example). ([pkg.go.dev](https://pkg.go.dev/github.com/modelcontextprotocol/go-sdk/examples/http))

Quick summary: stdio vs streamable HTTP (from the MCP spec and SDK docs/source)
- stdio: server and client communicate over the server process’s stdin/stdout (server usually run as a subprocess). This is the simplest deployment pattern and is recommended where a client can spawn the server locally. ([modelcontextprotocol.io](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-06-18/basic/transports))  
- streamable HTTP: uses HTTP with streaming semantics (POSTs for client→server and streaming responses / SSE for server→client); intended for networked/remote servers and web deployments. The SDK provides a StreamableHTTPHandler and server-side logic that routes messages to the appropriate HTTP stream. ([go.sdk.modelcontextprotocol.io](https://go.sdk.modelcontextprotocol.io/))  
- Recommendation/compatibility: the MCP spec notes both transports as standard; clients SHOULD support stdio when possible, but streamable HTTP is provided for remote/networked usage. The SDK implements both and includes examples for HTTP usage. ([modelcontextprotocol.io](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-06-18/basic/transports))

If you want next steps, I can:
- Open any of the items above (repo README, specific source files like streamable_server.go, or the spec page) and extract exact code snippets or protocol details.  
- Compare SDK API types/methods for StdioTransport vs StreamableTransport (list the relevant Go types/signatures).  
- Search for community discussion / deployment examples (e.g., how to launch stdio servers from common host apps).

Which would you like me to open or fetch next?

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="Read"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Read**</summary>
 `./backlog/api-server.md`

```text
1	# Local HTTP API server (`translate serve`) + OpenAPI/Swagger
2	
3	**Status**: P? — needs a spike
4	**Effort**: L
5	**Related**: `TODO.md` P? · `internal/engine/` · [raycast-extension.md](raycast-extension.md) · [../docs/raycast-extension.md](../docs/raycast-extension.md)
6	
7	## Context
8	
9	2026-07, surfaced while building the Raycast extension. Today every client (the
10	Raycast extension, script commands) **spawns the `translate` binary per call**. A
11	persistent local HTTP server (`translate serve`) with an OpenAPI/Swagger page was
12	proposed as "more usable." This doc records whether it is, and how.
13	
14	A related idea — folding `just raycast-dev` into the binary as `translate raycast
15	dev` — is **rejected** here (option C): it couples the Go CLI to the Node/`ray`
16	toolchain and the extension's on-disk location. Dev-tooling orchestration stays in
17	the `Justfile`. The useful subcommand is `translate serve`, not a `ray develop` wrapper.
18	
19	## Investigation
20	
21	- **Latency win is smaller than it looks.** Per-call spawn cost = config load +
22	  sqlite dict open + engine init. But LLM latency (~3–4 s) dominates, so a warm
23	  server mainly speeds up the **local dict/define** path, *not* LLM translation.
24	- **Streaming is the real win.** An HTTP server controls its own flushing → SSE
25	  (`text/event-stream`); clients consume via `fetch`/EventSource. Cleaner than the
26	  TTY / `--stream` / spawn path. (Caveat: upstream copilot-proxy still buffers claude
27	  `/v1/messages`, so visible streaming stays limited there regardless of transport.)
28	- **Multi-client + docs.** One loopback service → Raycast, Alfred, a browser
29	  extension, editor plugins, `curl`, a web UI. OpenAPI/Swagger documents it for all.
30	- **Sidesteps launchd-PATH** for request-time — the extension hits
31	  `http://127.0.0.1:PORT` instead of resolving/spawning the binary. But the *first*
32	  "start the daemon" still needs a spawn/LaunchAgent (PATH problem just moves).
33	- **Cheap to build.** Reuses `internal/engine` (pure Go, context-aware, TUI-free) as
34	  a thin HTTP adapter. stdlib `net/http` suffices (no framework dep). OpenAPI can be a
35	  hand-written `/openapi.json` + `go:embed`-ded Swagger UI, or generated
36	  (`swaggo/swag`, `danielgtaylor/huma`). Endpoints: `POST /v1/translate`,
37	  `POST /v1/define`, `GET /v1/history[?q=]`, SSE `GET /v1/translate/stream`.
38	
39	## Options considered
40	
41	| Option | What | Verdict |
42	|---|---|---|
43	| A. Keep spawn (current) | extension spawns `translate --json` / `--stream` per call | simplest, works, no daemon; per-call startup cost is small vs LLM latency |
44	| B. `translate serve` (HTTP + SSE + OpenAPI) | persistent loopback service; extension opt-in via a "server URL" pref, spawn fallback | best for streaming + multi-client + no-PATH; adds daemon lifecycle + security surface |
45	| C. `translate raycast dev` subcommand | binary shells out to npm/`ray` | rejected — couples Go CLI to Node toolchain + extension location; keep in Justfile |
46	
47	## Current blocker / open questions
48	
49	- **Daemon lifecycle**: how to keep it running — menu-bar app, a `LaunchAgent`, or the
50	  extension auto-starting it (which reintroduces the PATH problem for the first spawn)?
51	- **Security**: loopback-only bind is mandatory (history is personal data); consider a
52	  token for `/v1/history`.
53	- **Concurrent history writes**: server + CLI both appending `history.jsonl` — the
54	  store must be concurrency-safe (append + lock).
55	- **Port**: fixed default (collision risk) vs dynamic (needs discovery). Lean to a
56	  config'd default (e.g. `4155`) + preference.
57	- **Is it worth it vs spawn?** Only if we want a local translation *service* (streaming
58	  UX, multiple clients, web UI). The raw-latency argument is weak (LLM-dominated).
59	
60	## Decision (if any)
61	
62	`2026-07 deferred` — spawn mode works and ships. Revisit if we want a streaming-first
63	UX, multiple clients, or a web UI. If built: start minimal (stdlib `net/http`,
64	loopback, SSE, hand-written OpenAPI + embedded Swagger), extension **opt-in** with a
65	spawn fallback so non-server users are unaffected.
66	
67	## References
68	
69	- stdlib `net/http`; `go:embed` for the Swagger UI assets
70	- OpenAPI generators: https://github.com/swaggo/swag · https://github.com/danielgtaylor/huma
71	- Raycast integration + the streaming caveat: [../docs/raycast-extension.md](../docs/raycast-extension.md)
72
```
</details></tool-use>

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="Read"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Read**</summary>
 `./internal/engine/engine.go`

```text
1	// Package engine defines the translation backend abstraction and its
2	// implementations (LLM via OpenAI-compatible APIs, free web APIs, dictionaries).
3	//
4	// The engine layer is pure Go with context.Context and imports zero TUI code:
5	// the one-shot CLI and the Bubble Tea TUI drive the identical Engine values and
6	// diverge only at presentation.
7	package engine
8	
9	import "context"
10	
11	// Mode selects what kind of lookup a Request performs.
12	type Mode int
13	
14	const (
15		// ModeTranslate is register-aware, faithful translation (the default).
16		ModeTranslate Mode = iota
17		// ModeDict is a dictionary/definition lookup.
18		ModeDict
19	)
20	
21	// Request is the single input to every engine. A Source of "" or "auto" asks
22	// the engine to detect the source language.
23	type Request struct {
24		Text    string
25		Source  string // BCP-47-ish code or "auto"/""
26		Target  string // e.g. "en", "zh"
27		Mode    Mode
28		MaxAlts int    // cap on Alternatives; 0 => engine default
29		Stream  bool   // caller wants token streaming (LLM only; others ignore)
30		Model   string // optional per-request model override (LLM engines)
31		// ModelProvider scopes Model to one provider by name; other engines ignore
32		// the override (so a copilot model id never leaks to an Ollama fallback).
33		ModelProvider string
34		Preset        string // LLM prompt style: "" => concise (LLM translate only)
35		Extra         string // extra user instructions appended to the system prompt
36	
37		// Pair marks a bidirectional "pair" request: the text is written in one of
38		// two languages (PairHome/PairAway) and must be translated into the OTHER.
39		// The LLM translate prompt uses this to detect-and-route and, critically, to
40		// never echo the input unchanged. Target still carries the caller's routed
41		// best guess (for history/display and non-LLM engines).
42		Pair     bool
43		PairHome string // one pair language (e.g. the home target, "zh-TW")
44		PairAway string // the other pair language (e.g. "en")
45	
46		// Learn marks a language-learning request. It is bidirectional (implies Pair,
47		// with PairHome=native and PairAway=foreign) and adaptive: native-language input
48		// is translated-and-taught (glosses + examples), foreign-language input is
49		// grammar-corrected-and-explained. The LLM engine answers with a strict JSON
50		// object (parsed into TranslateResult.Learn) and, because the output is
51		// structured, always runs NON-STREAMING regardless of Stream.
52		Learn bool
53	
54		// Bilingual marks a whole-document bilingual ("doc mode") request: Segments
55		// carries the full ordered document (prose to translate + code shown only as
56		// context) and the LLM returns a JSON map of prose-segment number → translation.
57		// Context-aware and single-call. LLM engines only (google/dict ignore it), and
58		// like Learn it always runs NON-STREAMING.
59		Bilingual bool
60		Segments  []Segment
61	}
62	
63	// Segment is one unit of a Bilingual document request: a prose block to translate,
64	// or a command/code block included only as surrounding context (Code == true).
65	type Segment struct {
66		Text string
67		Code bool
68	}
69	
70	// TranslateResult is the "Marvin-lite" typed result. Every engine fills what it
71	// can; zero-value fields are simply absent. It is also the history record shape.
72	type TranslateResult struct {
73		Translation    string   `json:"translation"`
74		DetectedSource string   `json:"detected_source,omitempty"`
75		Target         string   `json:"target"`
76		Alternatives   []string `json:"alternatives,omitempty"`
77		Notes          string   `json:"notes,omitempty"`
78		Confidence     float64  `json:"confidence,omitempty"`
79	
80		// Warnings records non-fatal problems, e.g. a chain engine that failed before
81		// this (fallback) engine served the result — so a downgrade is never silent.
82		Warnings []string `json:"warnings,omitempty"`
83	
84		// Truncated is set when a streamed result ended before the model finished
85		// (no terminal marker, or stop_reason=="max_tokens"). The partial text is
86		// still returned so the caller can show it, but it must not be cached or
87		// persisted as if complete. Transient: not serialized.
88		Truncated bool `json:"-"`
89	
90		// Provenance — stamped by the producing engine / chain.
91		Engine string `json:"engine,omitempty"`
92		Model  string `json:"model,omitempty"`
93	
94		// Dictionary payload (Mode == ModeDict); nil for translate mode.
95		Dictionary *DictEntry `json:"dictionary,omitempty"`
96		// Learn payload (Request.Learn); nil outside learn mode. Translation still holds
97		// the main foreign-language sentence (the corrected sentence, or the translation)
98		// so copy/history/speak keep working unchanged; the structured extras ride here.
99		Learn *LearnResult `json:"learn,omitempty"`
100		// Bilingual is the doc-mode payload: prose-segment number (1-based, as numbered
101		// in the prompt) → translation. nil outside bilingual doc mode. Transient.
102		Bilingual map[int]string `json:"-"`
103		// Suggestions is a ranked "did you mean" list, set by the dictionary engine
104		// when no exact entry was found (Dictionary and Translation stay empty). The
105		// frontend decides how to present/select — the engine no longer auto-picks.
106		Suggestions []string `json:"suggestions,omitempty"`
107	
108		// SuggestDistance is the edit distance of the best entry in Suggestions (English
109		// fuzzy lookups only; 0 when unknown/not applicable, e.g. Chinese prefix matches).
110		// The smart-dict engine reads it to tell a likely typo from a match too far off.
111		// Transient: not serialized.
112		SuggestDistance int `json:"-"`
113	}
114	
115	// DictEntry is a dictionary lookup payload (populated by the dictionary engine).
116	type DictEntry struct {
117		Word      string    `json:"word"`
118		Phonetic  string    `json:"phonetic,omitempty"`
119		Meanings  []Meaning `json:"meanings,omitempty"`
120		SourceURL string    `json:"source_url,omitempty"`
121	}
122	
123	// Meaning groups definitions by part of speech.
124	type Meaning struct {
125		PartOfSpeech string       `json:"part_of_speech"`
126		Definitions  []Definition `json:"definitions"`
127		Synonyms     []string     `json:"synonyms,omitempty"`
128		Antonyms     []string     `json:"antonyms,omitempty"`
129	}
130	
131	// Definition is a single sense of a word.
132	type Definition struct {
133		Text    string `json:"definition"`
134		Example string `json:"example,omitempty"`
135	}
136	
137	// LearnResult is the structured payload for learn/tutor mode (Request.Learn). The
138	// model fills only the fields relevant to the auto-detected direction; all glosses,
139	// explanations, and notes are written in the native (home) language.
140	type LearnResult struct {
141		Direction   string         `json:"direction"`           // "teach" (native→foreign) | "correct" (foreign→native)
142		Original    string         `json:"original"`            // the user's input as received
143		Corrected   string         `json:"corrected,omitempty"` // correct-direction: the fixed foreign sentence
144		Translation string         `json:"translation"`         // teach: foreign translation; correct: native translation of intent
145		Notes       string         `json:"notes,omitempty"`     // short tip/encouragement, in the NATIVE language
146		Issues      []LearnIssue   `json:"issues,omitempty"`    // correct-direction grammar/usage fixes
147		Vocab       []LearnGloss   `json:"vocab,omitempty"`     // teach-direction glosses
148		Examples    []LearnExample `json:"examples,omitempty"`  // teach-direction usage examples
149	}
150	
151	// LearnIssue is one grammar/usage correction (correct-direction).
152	type LearnIssue struct {
153		Span        string `json:"span"`        // the problematic fragment of the input
154		Fix         string `json:"fix"`         // the corrected fragment
155		Explanation string `json:"explanation"` // why — in the NATIVE language
156	}
157	
158	// LearnGloss is one vocabulary explanation (teach-direction).
159	type LearnGloss struct {
160		Term     string `json:"term"`               // foreign word/term
161		Pos      string `json:"pos,omitempty"`      // part of speech
162		Phonetic string `json:"phonetic,omitempty"` // KK/IPA for English, pinyin for Chinese
163		Meaning  string `json:"meaning"`            // meaning in the NATIVE language
164	}
165	
166	// LearnExample is one usage example (teach-direction).
167	type LearnExample struct {
168		Foreign string `json:"foreign"` // example sentence in the foreign language
169		Native  string `json:"native"`  // its native-language translation
170	}
171	
172	// ChunkKind distinguishes streamed tokens from the terminal result/error.
173	type ChunkKind int
174	
175	const (
176		// ChunkToken is one streamed unit of translation text.
177		ChunkToken ChunkKind = iota
178		// ChunkDone carries the final structured result and ends the stream.
179		ChunkDone
180		// ChunkError carries a terminal error and ends the stream.
181		ChunkError
182	)
183	
184	// Chunk is one unit emitted on an engine's result channel.
185	type Chunk struct {
186		Kind   ChunkKind
187		Text   string           // token text (ChunkToken)
188		Result *TranslateResult // final result (ChunkDone)
189		Err    error            // terminal error (ChunkError)
190	}
191	
192	// Engine is the provider abstraction. Every method honors ctx cancellation.
193	//
194	// Translate returns a receive-only channel and MUST close it after sending
195	// exactly one terminal Chunk (ChunkDone or ChunkError). Streaming engines emit
196	// zero or more ChunkToken then one ChunkDone; non-streaming engines emit a
197	// single ChunkDone. This uniform "channel closed == finished" contract lets the
198	// TUI's self-resubscribing reader and the CLI's drain loop share one code path.
199	// The synchronous error return is only for immediate setup failures; all
200	// runtime/network errors flow as a terminal ChunkError.
201	type Engine interface {
202		Name() string
203		Translate(ctx context.Context, req Request) (<-chan Chunk, error)
204		Detect(ctx context.Context, text string) (string, error)
205		Available(ctx context.Context) bool
206		Supports(m Mode) bool
207	}
208	
209	// single is a helper for non-streaming engines: it returns a closed channel
210	// carrying exactly one terminal Chunk.
211	func single(res *TranslateResult, err error) <-chan Chunk {
212		ch := make(chan Chunk, 1)
213		if err != nil {
214			ch <- Chunk{Kind: ChunkError, Err: err}
215		} else {
216			ch <- Chunk{Kind: ChunkDone, Result: res}
217		}
218		close(ch)
219		return ch
220	}
221	
222	// Drain consumes an engine channel to completion, returning the final result.
223	// It streams tokens to onToken (if non-nil) as they arrive. Used by the
224	// one-shot CLI path; the TUI drives the channel directly via its Update loop.
225	func Drain(ch <-chan Chunk, onToken func(string)) (*TranslateResult, error) {
226		for c := range ch {
227			switch c.Kind {
228			case ChunkToken:
229				if onToken != nil {
230					onToken(c.Text)
231				}
232			case ChunkDone:
233				return c.Result, nil
234			case ChunkError:
235				return nil, c.Err
236			}
237		}
238		return nil, ErrNoResult
239	}
240
```
</details></tool-use>
