```text
===== engine test pattern (llm_test head) =====
package engine

import (
	"context"
	"net/http"
	"net/http/httptest"
	"strings"
	"testing"
)

// sseServer returns a test server that replies to any request with body, as an
// event stream. The whole body is written at once — the SSE reader scans it line
// by line regardless, so completeness detection is exercised identically.
func sseServer(t *testing.T, body string) *httptest.Server {
	t.Helper()
	srv := httptest.NewServer(http.HandlerFunc(func(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) {
		w.Header().Set("Content-Type", "text/event-stream")
		_, _ = w.Write([]byte(body))
	}))
	t.Cleanup(srv.Close)
	return srv
}

// runStream drives a streaming translation against a canned SSE body and returns
// the terminal result (via Drain).
func runStream(t *testing.T, model, body string) (*TranslateResult, error) {
	t.Helper()
	srv := sseServer(t, body)
	e := NewLLM(LLMConfig{Name: "test", BaseURL: srv.URL, Model: model})
	ch, err := e.Translate(context.Background(), Request{
		Text:   "hello",
		Source: "auto",
		Target: "zh",
		Mode:   ModeTranslate,
		Stream: true,
	})
	if err != nil {
		t.Fatalf("Translate setup error: %v", err)
	}
	return Drain(ch, nil)
}

// --- Anthropic (/v1/messages) completeness ---

const anthropicComplete = `event: message_start
data: {"type":"message_start"}

event: content_block_delta
data: {"type":"content_block_delta","index":0,"delta":{"type":"text_delta","text":"你好"}}

event: content_block_delta
data: {"type":"content_block_delta","index":0,"delta":{"type":"text_delta","text":"世界"}}

event: content_block_stop
data: {"type":"content_block_stop","index":0}

event: message_delta
data: {"type":"message_delta","delta":{"stop_reason":"end_turn"}}

event: message_stop
===== xdgpath =====
// Package xdgpath resolves XDG-style config/data/state directories for translate.
//
// It deliberately does NOT use github.com/adrg/xdg or os.UserConfigDir(): both
// return ~/Library/Application Support on macOS, which breaks this tool's
// ~/.config convention. Instead it honors the XDG_* environment variables and
// otherwise falls back to the freedesktop defaults (~/.config, ~/.local/share,
// ~/.local/state) on macOS AND Linux alike.
package xdgpath

import (
	"os"
	"path/filepath"
)

// app is the per-tool subdirectory used under each base dir.
const app = "translate"

// base returns $env if set, else $HOME/def.
func base(env, def string) string {
	if v := os.Getenv(env); v != "" {
		return v
	}
	home, err := os.UserHomeDir()
	if err != nil {
		home = "."
	}
	return filepath.Join(home, def)
}

// ConfigDir returns the translate config directory (e.g. ~/.config/translate).
func ConfigDir() string { return filepath.Join(base("XDG_CONFIG_HOME", ".config"), app) }

// DataDir returns the translate data directory (e.g. ~/.local/share/translate).
func DataDir() string { return filepath.Join(base("XDG_DATA_HOME", ".local/share"), app) }

// StateDir returns the translate state directory (e.g. ~/.local/state/translate).
func StateDir() string { return filepath.Join(base("XDG_STATE_HOME", ".local/state"), app) }

// CacheDir returns the translate cache directory (e.g. ~/.cache/translate). Used
// for regenerable artifacts like TTS audio; it is intentionally NOT created by
// EnsureDirs (cache is optional and created lazily on first write).
func CacheDir() string { return filepath.Join(base("XDG_CACHE_HOME", ".cache"), app) }

// ConfigFile returns the full path to config.toml.
func ConfigFile() string { return filepath.Join(ConfigDir(), "config.toml") }

// StateFile returns the full path to state.json.
func StateFile() string { return filepath.Join(StateDir(), "state.json") }

// HistoryFile returns the default history path (JSONL).
func HistoryFile() string { return filepath.Join(DataDir(), "history.jsonl") }

// EnsureDirs creates the config, data, and state directories (0700) if missing.
func EnsureDirs() error {
	for _, d := range []string{ConfigDir(), DataDir(), StateDir()} {
		if err := os.MkdirAll(d, 0o700); err != nil {
			return err
		}
	}
	return nil
}
```
</details></tool-use>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
I'm verifying that the LLM engine's Translate method properly handles context cancellation through the channel model—I can see the ctx.Done() checks at lines 612 and 665, and the buffered channel with goroutine setup. Since the engine already respects context cancellation, passing r.Context() to the Service will correctly propagate client disconnects through the SSE handler.

Now I'm thinking through the architectural recommendation. The requirement asks me to choose between putting server code in the cmd package or lifting the builders into a shared internal package like internal/appcore. I need to weigh the tradeoffs carefully and make a concrete recommendation based on the codebase structure. I need to update all the call sites in cmd to use the qualified names from the new package, and I'm settling on `internal/appcore` as the home for the builders and Service type. The dependency graph flows cleanly: appcore sits above engine, config, store, and lang, then both server and mcpserver import from appcore rather than duplicating the wiring logic.

One thing I need to verify is whether moving the builder functions will break the TUI's buildEngineSet in cmd/build.go, which currently calls googleFromConfig, dictFromConfig, and smartDictFromConfig directly.

If I move all these builders to internal/appcore/build.go and export them, then buildEngineSet stays in cmd but now imports appcore and calls the exported versions instead. That dependency direction works fine.

The refactor plan is to create internal/appcore/build.go with all the builder functions exported (BuildEngine, BuildAutoChain, LLMFromProvider, DictFromConfig, SmartDictFromConfig, SmartAutoFromConfig, GoogleFromConfig, LearnEngineFromConfig), then update cmd to import and use them.

Now I'm thinking about the Service struct — it needs to hold the config, resolved state, and a warm primary engine.  The key insight is that resolution happens once at startup (no per-request flags like in the CLI), but clients can still override source/target per request, which the engine's Translate method already handles through the Request object.

For v1, I'm keeping the scope tight: build one default engine at startup and allow per-request overrides only for fields the engine already supports (source, target, preset, instructions, maxAlts, stream, and model via the LLM engine). Provider or engine switching per request is deferred as a future enhancement since it would require building or caching multiple engines.

Now I'm sketching out the Service structure: NewService takes a config and options (like NoHistory and Mode), resolves the configuration, builds both the translate and define engines, opens the history store if needed, and captures the home/away language pair. Then I'm defining the method signatures, starting with TranslateParams as the standard request shape.

Defining the core Service methods... Translate and TranslateStream both use a shared helper that resolves the effective target language pair, defaults the source to the config's default_source setting, calls the engine to translate, and records the result to the store. I'm extracting the toRecord mapping function into appcore so both the CLI and server can use it consistently to convert engine results into store records, though the server skips the last-pair persistence since it's stateless.

Now I need to move effectiveTarget into appcore as well—it's a small helper that resolves the language pair—and then tackle the concurrency issue with the history store. The jsonl file uses O_APPEND writes protected by an in-process mutex, which works fine for single-process access, but when both the server and CLI write to the same file, I need to ensure atomicity. POSIX guarantees atomic writes under PIPE_BUF (4096 bytes) with O_APPEND, so short JSON lines are safe, but longer inputs could exceed that threshold and interleave.

For the solution, I'll recommend documenting the O_APPEND atomicity caveat for v1, then optionally add advisory file locking around the append-and-read operation in the jsonl store using syscall.Flock on Unix (with a no-op or build-tagged fallback for Windows). I'll also note that the existing in-process mutex already covers the common case, and suggest that running the server as the single writer—since Raycast clients talk to the server rather than spawning the CLI directly—makes double-writes rare in practice.

Now I'm defining the Server struct in the config...

Adding struct fields... Defining the server struct with graceful shutdown, handlers for translate/define/history/stream endpoints, SSE support, and an embedded OpenAPI spec with Swagger UI. Using Go 1.22's net/http ServeMux with method+path patterns instead of a framework.

The translate handler decodes JSON, calls the service, and returns results; define works similarly; history supports search or recent queries with token auth; and the stream endpoint uses GET with query params for EventSource compatibility, emitting token events followed by a terminal done or error event.

Writing SSE handler... Finishing the error handling and then checking the context cancellation flow through the service layer to confirm it propagates all the way to the engine's goroutine. I need to make sure the Truncated warning gets emitted as a separate event since it's transient state, not part of the final JSON result. There's a note about copilot-proxy buffering that means streaming will still be bursty even with this setup. Now I'm structuring the error response as a JSON envelope. I need to check main.go to see how the context and signal handling are already set up, so I can integrate the graceful shutdown properly into the command.
</details></think>

```text
185	func (e *LLMEngine) Translate(ctx context.Context, req Request) (<-chan Chunk, error) {
```

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
So the context flow is already set up—`main.go` handles Ctrl-C with `signal.NotifyContext`, and that cancellable context gets passed through to the serve command's RunE. The LLM engine's Translate method respects context cancellation, so when a client disconnects, the abort signal propagates all the way upstream through the request context. I just need to wire up graceful shutdown for the serve command using that same context, and handle both SIGINT and SIGTERM.

For the MCP server, I'm weighing the options: the official Go SDK is actively maintained but adds dependencies, the community SDK has even more transitive deps, or I could hand-roll JSON-RPC-2.0 over stdio for the three tools—about 200-300 lines with zero external dependencies. Given the project's philosophy of staying stdlib-only and hand-writing integrations like the OpenAPI spec, hand-rolling the MCP server makes the most sense. I'll present all three approaches but recommend the hand-rolled option as the best fit for this codebase's design principles. The protocol is genuinely implementable in around 250 lines with clear specs, so hand-rolling makes sense given the project's scope and philosophy, with the official SDK available as a documented upgrade path. For transport, stdio via the `translate mcp` subcommand should be the v1 priority since it's what Claude Desktop and similar tools use—it's secure, requires no networking setup, and keeps personal data within the process. Streamable-HTTP can wait for later if needed.

I'm structuring the MCP server as an internal package that handles JSON-RPC over stdio, with a tool registry for the three operations and handlers that delegate to the same core service methods.

Now I need to define the tool schemas: `translate` takes text plus optional source and target language parameters and returns the translation along with any structured alternatives; `define` accepts a word and returns formatted definition text; `history` takes an optional query and limit to return recent or filtered records. There's a question about reusing the dictionary rendering logic that currently lives in the CLI layer—I should either move that formatter into the core service or inline it for the MCP response. use it in both places. For the phased rollout, Phase 0 is a pure refactor moving builders and the toRecord function into appcore with a new Service type and tests — this ships independently without changing CLI behavior. Phase 1 adds the `translate serve` command with REST JSON endpoints for translate, define, and history, plus server config and graceful shutdown. Phase 2 will handle SSE and OpenAPI.

The key insight is that Phase 4 (the MCP stdio server) only depends on Phase 0's Service, so it can ship in parallel with or independently of the HTTP phases. For testing, I'm planning table-driven tests for the Service methods using a fake engine implementation.

I'll set up the Service with dependency injection—an internal constructor that accepts a prebuilt engine and store to avoid network calls during tests, while the public NewService wires everything from config. For the HTTP handlers, I'm using httptest to mock requests and responses, following the pattern already established in the engine tests. SSE testing...

Actually, httptest.ResponseRecorder does implement http.Flusher, so I can test the SSE handler by feeding it a stub engine that emits tokens and verifying the recorded body contains the right event lines ending with `event: done`. For real streaming behavior with actual flushing, I'll spin up an httptest.NewServer and read the response body line by line. For MCP, I'm running a smoke test of the JSON-RPC handshake by piping a sequence of JSON lines (initialize, tools/list, tools/call) through the stdio loop using bytes.Buffer for input and output, then table-testing each tool through the dispatch function.

The test coverage with `go test ./cmd/... ./internal/...` already hits all the new packages under internal/, and since the cmd layer stays thin with logic pushed into internal packages, I don't need to add tests there or change the Justfile. A few things to keep in mind: daemon lifecycle management is out of scope for this binary (that's handled separately by LaunchAgent/menubar), SSE streaming through copilot-proxy has inherent buffering limits I should set expectations around, and cross-process history writes would benefit from flock hardening or a single-writer pattern to avoid contention.

For the API design, per-request engine/provider overrides aren't supported in v1 since we're running a warm single engine, and I'm accepting the risk of hand-rolled JSON-RPC against the MCP spec rather than pulling in the official SDK. Token storage should use token_env rather than embedding it in config.toml, port 4155 is the config default (discovery isn't implemented yet), the serve command stays stateless and doesn't persist the last provider pair to avoid conflicts with the CLI, and bilingual/learn endpoints are deferred to future versions. For the OpenAPI spec, I'm going with a hand-written /openapi.json served as a static file—either embedded as a Go string or loaded via go:embed with the right content type.

Embedding Swagger UI with vendored assets... cmd and Service both use it. For cmd/define.go specifically, I'd move the logic that selects between plain and smart define modes into appcore as DefineEngine, so both the command and the Service layer can call the same builder without duplicating that selection logic.

Now I have enough detail to write out the full plan with concrete signatures and file references.
</details></think>

---

# Implementation Architecture: `translate serve` (HTTP + OpenAPI/Swagger) + `translate mcp` (MCP)

I read the actual sources; every recommendation below is grounded in the current code. The single most important finding that shapes the whole design: the engine *builders* (`buildEngine`, `llmFromProvider`, `dictFromConfig`, `buildAutoChain`, `smartDictFromConfig`, `smartAutoFromConfig`, `googleFromConfig`, `learnEngineFromConfig`) live in package `cmd` (`cmd/build.go`) and are only coupled to `internal/config` + `internal/engine` — except `buildEngineSet`, which is the one function that imports `internal/tui`. That asymmetry makes the layering fix clean.

---

## 1. Package layout & the layering fix (decisive recommendation)

**Recommendation: Option (b) — lift the builders into a new shared package `internal/appcore`.** Reject Option (a) (server-in-`cmd`): the SSE/OpenAPI/MCP code is substantial and testable in isolation; burying it in `cmd` (which has *zero* test files today) forfeits `httptest`/table tests and re-couples presentation to transport.

New dependency graph (arrows = "imports"):

```
internal/engine      (pure; unchanged)
internal/config      (unchanged except a new [server] table)
internal/store       (unchanged, + optional flock hardening)
internal/lang        (unchanged)
        ▲   ▲   ▲   ▲
        └───┴─┬─┴───┘
   internal/appcore        NEW — engine builders + Service (warm engine + store)
        ▲          ▲
   internal/server   internal/mcpserver     NEW transports; each imports appcore
        ▲          ▲
        └────┬─────┘
           cmd          imports appcore + server + mcpserver + tui (buildEngineSet stays here)
```

`internal/appcore` imports only `config`, `engine`, `store`, `lang` — **never** `tui` or `cmd`, so both `cmd` and the two server packages can import it with no cycle.

**New files:**
- `internal/appcore/build.go` — the 8 builders, moved out of `cmd/build.go` and exported.
- `internal/appcore/service.go` — the `Service` type (section 2).
- `internal/appcore/record.go` — `ToRecord` (moved from `cmd/root.go:364`) + the `effectiveTarget` pair-routing helper (copied from `cmd/root.go:268`).
- `internal/appcore/service_test.go`, `internal/appcore/build_test.go`.
- `internal/server/{server.go,handlers.go,sse.go,openapi.go,errors.go}` + `internal/server/swagger/` (embedded assets) + `_test.go`.
- `internal/mcpserver/{mcp.go,tools.go}` + `_test.go`.
- `cmd/serve.go`, `cmd/mcp.go` (thin cobra wrappers).

**Refactor steps (Phase 0, pure move, no behavior change):**

1. Move these from `cmd/build.go` → `internal/appcore/build.go`, exporting each: `buildEngine`→`BuildEngine`, `buildAutoChain`→`BuildAutoChain`, `llmFromProvider`→`LLMFromProvider`, `googleFromConfig`→`GoogleFromConfig`, `dictFromConfig`→`DictFromConfig`, `smartDictFromConfig`→`SmartDictFromConfig`, `smartAutoFromConfig`→`SmartAutoFromConfig`, `learnEngineFromConfig`→`LearnEngineFromConfig`. They already take `config.Resolved`/`*config.Config` only — no signature changes.
2. Move `toRecord` (`cmd/root.go:364`) → `appcore.ToRecord`; move the body of `effectiveTarget` (`cmd/root.go:268`, which calls `lang.PairTarget`) into an unexported `appcore.effectiveTarget` (drop the `debug.Logf` line, or keep `internal/debug` — it's dependency-free).
3. Add `appcore.DefineEngine(res config.Resolved, plain, smart bool) engine.Engine` capturing the selection logic in `cmd/define.go:72` (`defineEngine`) so CLI and Service agree on the exact same define engine.
4. Update call sites (the only churn):
   - `cmd/build.go`: keep `buildEngineSet` (it imports `tui`); rewrite its body to call `appcore.GoogleFromConfig/DictFromConfig/SmartDictFromConfig`.
   - `cmd/root.go:191` `buildEngine`→`appcore.BuildEngine`; `:402/:206/:248/:409` `learnEngineFromConfig`/`llmFromProvider`→`appcore.*`; `recordAndRemember` (`:356`) uses `appcore.ToRecord`.
   - `cmd/define.go`: `defineEngine` becomes a one-liner `return appcore.DefineEngine(res, flagDefinePlain, flagDefineSmart)`.
5. `just check` (`go vet . ./cmd/... ./internal/...` + build) must stay green. This phase ships alone with identical CLI behavior.

---

## 2. Shared service core (`internal/appcore.Service`)

Builds the engine **once** (warm) and holds the store for the process lifetime. One instance is shared across all HTTP handler goroutines and the MCP loop.

```go
type Service struct {
    cfg     *config.Config
    res     config.Resolved   // resolved once at startup (no per-request flags)
    trans   engine.Engine     // warm translate engine (BuildEngine)
    define  engine.Engine     // warm define engine (DefineEngine)
    store   store.Store       // shared history; nil when disabled/--no-history
    home    string            // resolved default target (pair "home")
    away    string            // resolved pair_with ("away"); "" when pair off
    pair    bool
}

type Options struct { NoHistory bool }

// NewService resolves cfg with empty overrides, warms both engines, opens the store.
func NewService(cfg *config.Config, opt Options) (*Service, error)

// newService(trans, define engine.Engine, st store.Store, ...) — unexported ctor for tests (no network).

type Params struct {
    Text, Source, Target, Preset, Instructions, Model string
    MaxAlts int
    Pair    *bool   // nil => use config default; non-nil => per-request override
}

func (s *Service) Translate(ctx context.Context, p Params) (*engine.TranslateResult, error)
func (s *Service) TranslateStream(ctx context.Context, p Params, onToken func(string)) (*engine.TranslateResult, error)
func (s *Service) Define(ctx context.Context, word string) (*engine.TranslateResult, error)
func (s *Service) HistoryRecent(ctx context.Context, limit int) ([]store.Record, error)
func (s *Service) HistorySearch(ctx context.Context, query string, limit int) ([]store.Record, error)
func (s *Service) Close() error   // closes the store
```

Internals — `Translate`/`TranslateStream` share a `buildRequest(p)` helper that mirrors `oneShot` (`cmd/root.go:462`) and `effectiveTarget` (`:268`):
- default `Source` ← `s.res.Source` (usually `"auto"`), `Target` ← `p.Target` or `s.home`;
- when pair is on, `req.Target = appcore.effectiveTarget(home, away, text)` and set `req.Pair/PairHome/PairAway` (so the LLM prompt never echoes input) exactly as the CLI does;
- pass `Preset`, `Extra=Instructions`, `Model`, `MaxAlts`, `Stream` through;
- call `s.trans.Translate(ctx, req)`, then `engine.Drain(ch, onToken)` (reuse the existing helper at `engine.go:225`);
- on success **and** `!Truncated`, record via `s.store.Add(ctx, appcore.ToRecord(res, text, source, target))`. Do **not** call `saveLastPair` — the server must stay out of `state.json` so it never fights the CLI's remembered pair.

`Define` calls `s.define.Translate(ctx, engine.Request{Text: word, Mode: engine.ModeDict})` then `Drain(ch, nil)` — identical to `runDefine` (`cmd/define.go:44`) minus printing.

Key reuse: nothing here re-implements engine logic; it re-wires the exact CLI code paths (`Drain`, `ToRecord`, `effectiveTarget`, `buildRequest≈oneShot`).

---

## 3. HTTP server (`translate serve`)

**Router:** stdlib `net/http.ServeMux` with Go 1.22+ method+path patterns (`mux.HandleFunc("POST /v1/translate", …)`) — no framework, consistent with the codebase.

**cobra command** `cmd/serve.go` → `newServeCmd()`, registered in `cmd/root.go:95`. Flags (override `[server]` config): `--port int`, `--bind string`, `--token string`, `--no-history bool`, `--enable-mcp bool` (Phase 5). RunE:
```
cfg,_ ,_ := config.Load()
sc := cfg.ResolveServer(serveFlags)      // small helper: flags > [server] > defaults
svc,_ := appcore.NewService(cfg, appcore.Options{NoHistory: sc.NoHistory})
srv := server.New(svc, sc)               // *http.Server with the mux
run(cmd.Context(), srv, svc)             // graceful shutdown
```

**Endpoints** (reusing `engine.TranslateResult` — already fully JSON-tagged, `engine.go:72` — as the response body verbatim):

| Method + path | Request JSON | Response |
|---|---|---|
| `POST /v1/translate` | `{text, source?, target?, preset?, instructions?, model?, max_alts?, pair?}` | `TranslateResult` |
| `POST /v1/define` | `{word}` | `TranslateResult` (with `dictionary`/`suggestions`) |
| `GET /v1/history?q=&limit=` | — | `[]store.Record` |
| `GET /v1/translate/stream?text=&target=…` | query params (EventSource is GET-only) | `text/event-stream` |
| `GET /openapi.json`, `GET /docs`, `GET /healthz` | — | spec / Swagger UI / `{"status":"ok"}` |

Handlers decode with `json.NewDecoder(r.Body).Decode`, validate (empty `text`→422), call the `Service`, and `json.NewEncoder(w).Encode(res)`. History is guarded by the bearer token (section 7).

**SSE handler** (`internal/server/sse.go`):
```go
flusher, ok := w.(http.Flusher)            // 503 if not
w.Header().Set("Content-Type", "text/event-stream")
w.Header().Set("Cache-Control", "no-cache")
ctx := r.Context()                          // cancels on client disconnect
onToken := func(t string){ writeSSE(w,"token",t); flusher.Flush() }
res, err := svc.TranslateStream(ctx, params, onToken)
if err != nil { writeSSE(w,"error", jsonErr(err)); flusher.Flush(); return }
if res.Truncated { writeSSE(w,"warning","result was truncated (max_tokens)") }
writeSSE(w,"done", mustJSON(res)); flusher.Flush()
```
- **Client-disconnect cancellation is already wired end-to-end**: `r.Context()` → `Service` ctx → `engine.Engine.Translate(ctx,…)` → the LLM goroutine's `case <-ctx.Done()` (confirmed at `internal/engine/llm.go:612` and `:665`), which aborts the upstream request. No extra plumbing needed.
- **Truncated warning preservation**: `TranslateResult.Truncated` is `json:"-"` (transient, `engine.go:88`), so it would vanish in the `done` payload — the handler must emit it as an explicit `warning` event, as above.
- **Streaming caveat (honor it, don't over-promise):** copilot-proxy buffers Claude `/v1/messages`, so visible token cadence stays coarse for Claude models regardless of SSE — this is upstream and transport-independent. Document it on `/docs` and in the backlog note; SSE's real win is dict/google/OpenAI-style providers and multi-client fan-out.

**Error model:** one envelope `{"error":{"code":"bad_request","message":"…"}}` via `writeError(w, status, code, msg)` in `errors.go`. Status map: 400 malformed JSON · 422 empty text/word · 401 missing/invalid token · 404 unknown route · 405 wrong method · 500 engine error. `engine.ErrEmptyInput` (`llm.go:188`) maps to 422.

**Graceful shutdown:** reuse the already-cancellable `cmd.Context()` (main wires `signal.NotifyContext(…, os.Interrupt)` at `main.go:15`; add `syscall.SIGTERM` in the serve command via its own `signal.NotifyContext`). On `ctx.Done()`: `srv.Shutdown(shutCtx)` with a ~5 s timeout, then `svc.Close()`. Log the bound address on startup.

---

## 4. OpenAPI + Swagger UI

**Recommendation: hand-written `/openapi.json` + `go:embed`-ded Swagger UI.** This matches the backlog's explicit preference and the zero-framework philosophy. Reject `swaggo/swag` (needs code annotations + a `go generate` step + a build-time binary) and `huma` (a full framework that would restructure every handler and add a large dep tree — contrary to the lean `go.mod`).

- **Spec:** author an OpenAPI 3.1 document as `internal/server/openapi.json`, `go:embed`-ded and served at `GET /openapi.json` with `Content-Type: application/json`. Keep the `components.schemas` for `TranslateResult`/`DictEntry`/`Record` in sync by hand (small surface; a `TestOpenAPISchemaMatchesStruct` reflection test can guard the top-level field names against the structs to catch drift).
- **Swagger UI:** vendor a pinned `swagger-ui-dist` (only `swagger-ui.css`, `swagger-ui-bundle.js`, and a small `index.html` whose `SwaggerUIBundle({url:'/openapi.json'})`) under `internal/server/swagger/`, `go:embed`-ded and served at `GET /docs` via `http.FileServerFS`. No CDN (loopback/offline-friendly). Note: adds ~1.5 MB to the binary; if that matters, gate behind a `swaggerui` build tag with a plain-text fallback at `/docs`.

---

## 5. MCP server (`translate mcp`)

**Recommendation: add a separate `translate mcp` stdio subcommand, and hand-roll JSON-RPC 2.0 over stdio (no SDK) for v1.**

Rationale:
- **Transport — stdio first.** Claude Desktop/Code/Cursor *spawn the binary* and speak newline-delimited JSON-RPC over stdio. This is the highest-value integration and it **sidesteps the entire HTTP security surface** (no port, no bind, no token; personal history never leaves the process's stdio pipe; the child inherits the user's env for provider keys). Streamable-HTTP `/mcp` on the serve port is a *later* add (Phase 5), gated by `[server].enable_mcp`, reusing the same tool handlers.
- **SDK vs hand-roll.** Only 3 tools, all mapping 1:1 to `Service` methods. The MCP stdio handshake is small and well-specified: `initialize` → `initialized` (notification) → `tools/list` → `tools/call`, framed as newline-delimited JSON-RPC 2.0. Hand-rolling is ~200–300 lines, adds **zero dependencies**, and mirrors the deliberate "hand-written OpenAPI" decision — consistent with the lean `go.mod` (no MCP dep today). Trade-off stated honestly: the official `github.com/modelcontextprotocol/go-sdk` handles protocol-version negotiation, content typing, resources/prompts/sampling, and future spec churn for you; hand-rolling risks spec-drift as MCP evolves. Mitigation: keep the JSON-RPC/dispatch layer isolated in `internal/mcpserver/mcp.go` so swapping in the official SDK later is a contained change. `mark3labs/mcp-go` is not recommended (community SDK, heavier dep tree, no advantage over the official SDK if we ever take a dep).

**Package `internal/mcpserver`:**
- `mcp.go`: JSON-RPC types; `Serve(ctx, in io.Reader, out io.Writer, svc *appcore.Service)` running a `bufio.Scanner` read-loop (one JSON message per line) → `dispatch(method, params)` → write response. Making it take `io.Reader/io.Writer` (not hard-coded os.Stdin/os.Stdout) is what enables the smoke test in section 9.
- `tools.go`: the tool registry + handlers.

**Tool schemas** (JSON Schema `inputSchema`; each handler calls the shared `Service`):

| Tool | inputSchema | Returns (`content: [{type:"text",…}]`) |
|---|---|---|
| `translate` | `{text*:string, target?:string, source?:string}` | translation text; alternatives/notes appended. Optionally `structuredContent` = the `TranslateResult` JSON. |
| `define` | `{word*:string}` | plain-formatted definition (or the `TranslateResult` JSON as text) |
| `history` | `{query?:string, limit?:integer}` | recent/matching records as a compact text list |

For define/history text, returning the `TranslateResult`/`[]Record` **JSON as a text block** is the pragmatic v1 choice (LLM clients parse it fine) — avoids pulling `renderDict` (`cmd/define.go:84`) out of `cmd`. If richer prose is wanted later, move a plain renderer into `appcore`.

`cmd/mcp.go` → `newMcpCmd()`: `RunE` builds `appcore.NewService(cfg, {})` then `mcpserver.Serve(cmd.Context(), os.Stdin, os.Stdout, svc)`. Registered at `cmd/root.go:95`. **Do not** write logs to stdout (it's the JSON-RPC channel) — send any diagnostics to stderr.

---

## 6. Config schema (`[server]` table)

Add to `Config` (`internal/config/config.go:17`):
```go
Server Server `toml:"server"`

type Server struct {
    Port      int    `toml:"port"`                 // default 4155
    Bind      string `toml:"bind"`                 // default "127.0.0.1"
    Token     string `toml:"token,omitempty"`      // optional inline bearer token
    TokenEnv  string `toml:"token_env,omitempty"`  // env var holding the token (preferred)
    EnableMCP bool   `toml:"enable_mcp"`           // expose /mcp (Phase 5); default false
}
```
In `Default()` (`config.go:178`): `Server: Server{Port: 4155, Bind: "127.0.0.1"}`. **Bump `SchemaVersion` 1→2** (`config.go:43`) with a history comment (`2 — [server] table (translate serve)`). No migration code required: the auto-upgrade path at `cmd/root.go:146-158` already re-saves and materializes additive defaults ("Schema bumps so far are additive…"). Add a small `Config.ResolveServer(flags)` helper (flags > `[server]` > defaults) used by `cmd/serve.go`.

---

## 7. Security

- **Loopback bind is mandatory and fail-closed.** Default `Bind:"127.0.0.1"`. If a resolved bind is non-loopback (not `127.0.0.0/8`/`::1`), **refuse to start** unless a token is configured (require intent + auth before exposing personal history on a LAN). Enforce with a `net.ParseIP(...).IsLoopback()` check in `server.New`.
- **Bearer token.** Resolve token from `--token` > `[server].token` > `os.Getenv([server].token_env)` (recommend `token_env` so the secret stays out of `config.toml`). When set, require `Authorization: Bearer <token>` (constant-time compare via `crypto/subtle.ConstantTimeCompare`) on **`/v1/history`** always (personal data). Optionally guard all `/v1/*` when a `require_token_all` intent is desired; `/healthz`, `/openapi.json`, `/docs` stay open on loopback.
- **MCP stdio sidesteps all of this**: no listener, no port, no token — the transport is a private stdio pipe to a parent process the user already trusts, and history never crosses a network boundary. This is the security argument for shipping MCP as stdio first.

---

## 8. Incremental delivery / phasing

- **Phase 0 — Refactor to `internal/appcore` + `Service` (no behavior change).** Ships alone; de-risks everything; existing CLI byte-identical. Gated by `just check`/`just test`.
- **Phase 1 — `translate serve` REST JSON** (`/v1/translate`, `/v1/define`, `/v1/history`, `/healthz`) + `[server]` config + loopback enforcement + token + graceful shutdown. Ships usable (Raycast/Alfred/curl can hit JSON).
- **Phase 2 — SSE** `/v1/translate/stream`. Independent increment on top of Phase 1.
- **Phase 3 — OpenAPI + Swagger** `/openapi.json` + `/docs`. Pure add.
- **Phase 4 — `translate mcp` (stdio, hand-rolled).** **Depends only on Phase 0**, *not* on Phases 1–3 — can ship in parallel with, or even before, the HTTP work. This is worth calling out: MCP is the cheapest high-value deliverable once `Service` exists.
- **Phase 5 (optional) — `/mcp` streamable-HTTP** on the serve port, gated by `[server].enable_mcp`, reusing Phase 4's tool handlers.

Independently shippable: Phase 0, Phase 4 (given 0), and each of 1/2/3 layer additively.

---

## 9. Testing strategy

- **`appcore.Service`** (`service_test.go`): table tests with a **stub `engine.Engine`** returning canned `Chunk`s (translate: tokens + `ChunkDone`; define: single `ChunkDone` with a `Dictionary`; error: `ChunkError`). Construct via the unexported `newService(stubEng, stubDef, memStore, cfg)`. Assert target routing (pair on/off via `lang.PairTarget`), history recording, and `Truncated` suppression of the record. Use a temp-file `store.OpenJSONL(t.TempDir()+"/h.jsonl")` for real store round-trips.
- **HTTP handlers** (`internal/server/*_test.go`): follow the existing `httptest` pattern already used in `internal/engine/llm_test.go` (`httptest.NewServer`, line 14). For JSON endpoints use `httptest.NewRecorder` + the mux; assert status + decoded body. For token: assert 401 without header, 200 with.
- **SSE**: `httptest.ResponseRecorder` implements `http.Flusher`, so a recorder + stub engine that emits N tokens lets you assert the body contains `event: token` × N and a terminal `event: done`; add one `httptest.NewServer` test that reads the response body line-by-line to exercise real flushing and a `r.Context()`-cancel path (cancel the client, assert the stub's ctx fired).
- **MCP** (`internal/mcpserver/mcp_test.go`): drive `Serve(ctx, in, out, svc)` with `in := bytes.NewBufferString(...)` containing the `initialize` → `tools/list` → `tools/call` line sequence and a `bytes.Buffer` out; assert the handshake result advertises 3 tools and that `tools/call` returns the stubbed translation. Table-test each tool via `dispatch`.
- **Coverage/CI**: all three new packages sit under `internal/`, so the existing globs (`go test ./cmd/... ./internal/...`, `go vet …` — `Justfile` lines 36/44) pick them up with **no Justfile change**. `cmd/serve.go`/`cmd/mcp.go` stay thin (logic lives in `internal/`), consistent with `cmd` having no tests today.

---

## 10. Risks / open questions to surface

1. **Cross-process history writes (server + CLI).** `store/jsonl.go` uses an in-process `sync.Mutex` only (line 20), no flock. Two processes `O_APPEND` to `history.jsonl`: POSIX guarantees atomic appends only under `PIPE_BUF` (4096 B) — long inputs could interleave. Options: (a) accept it and document (clients that use the server don't also spawn the CLI, so collisions are rare); (b) **harden `jsonlStore.Add`** with an advisory `syscall.Flock(fd, LOCK_EX)` on the open file (unix), behind a build tag with a Windows no-op. Recommend (b) as a small, contained follow-up; needs your call since it touches a shared file. `Recent`/`Search` re-read the whole file each call (`readAll`, line 61) — fine for personal-scale history, but note it's O(file) per request under a server.
2. **Per-request engine/provider switching is out of scope for v1.** The Service warms **one** engine at startup; per-request overrides are limited to what the warm engine already honors via `engine.Request` (source, target, preset, instructions, `model`/`model_provider`, max_alts). Confirm that's acceptable, or we add an engine cache keyed by `{engine,provider,model}` later.
3. **copilot-proxy buffering** caps *visible* Claude streaming regardless of SSE (upstream). Set expectations on `/docs`.
4. **MCP spec drift** with hand-rolled JSON-RPC vs taking the official Go SDK dep — accepted for v1 (isolated in `mcp.go` for easy later swap). Confirm the minimal-dep stance holds for MCP.
5. **Daemon lifecycle is explicitly out of scope** (backlog open question): the binary offers `serve`; keeping it running (LaunchAgent / menu-bar / extension auto-start) is separate and re-introduces the first-spawn PATH problem. Not solved here.
6. **Port 4155 default** — collision risk; no discovery mechanism in v1 (config'd default per the backlog).
7. **Bilingual / learn have no API/MCP surface in v1** (structured, non-streaming, CLI-shaped). Note as future.
8. **Token in `config.toml`** (0600) vs `token_env` — recommend `token_env`; confirm.
9. **Swagger UI binary-size** (~1.5 MB embedded) — acceptable, or gate behind a build tag?

---

# Implementation Architecture: `translate serve` (HTTP + OpenAPI/Swagger) + `translate mcp` (MCP)

I read the actual sources; every recommendation below is grounded in the current code. The single most important finding that shapes the whole design: the engine *builders* (`buildEngine`, `llmFromProvider`, `dictFromConfig`, `buildAutoChain`, `smartDictFromConfig`, `smartAutoFromConfig`, `googleFromConfig`, `learnEngineFromConfig`) live in package `cmd` (`cmd/build.go`) and are only coupled to `internal/config` + `internal/engine` — except `buildEngineSet`, which is the one function that imports `internal/tui`. That asymmetry makes the layering fix clean.

---

## 1. Package layout &amp; the layering fix (decisive recommendation)

**Recommendation: Option (b) — lift the builders into a new shared package `internal/appcore`.** Reject Option (a) (server-in-`cmd`): the SSE/OpenAPI/MCP code is substantial and testable in isolation; burying it in `cmd` (which has *zero* test files today) forfeits `httptest`/table tests and re-couples presentation to transport.

New dependency graph (arrows = "imports"):

```
internal/engine      (pure; unchanged)
internal/config      (unchanged except a new [server] table)
internal/store       (unchanged, + optional flock hardening)
internal/lang        (unchanged)
        ▲   ▲   ▲   ▲
        └───┴─┬─┴───┘
   internal/appcore        NEW — engine builders + Service (warm engine + store)
        ▲          ▲
   internal/server   internal/mcpserver     NEW transports; each imports appcore
        ▲          ▲
        └────┬─────┘
           cmd          imports appcore + server + mcpserver + tui (buildEngineSet stays here)
```

`internal/appcore` imports only `config`, `engine`, `store`, `lang` — **never** `tui` or `cmd`, so both `cmd` and the two server packages can import it with no cycle.

**New files:**
- `internal/appcore/build.go` — the 8 builders, moved out of `cmd/build.go` and exported.
- `internal/appcore/service.go` — the `Service` type (section 2).
- `internal/appcore/record.go` — `ToRecord` (moved from `cmd/root.go:364`) + the `effectiveTarget` pair-routing helper (copied from `cmd/root.go:268`).
- `internal/appcore/service_test.go`, `internal/appcore/build_test.go`.
- `internal/server/{server.go,handlers.go,sse.go,openapi.go,errors.go}` + `internal/server/swagger/` (embedded assets) + `_test.go`.
- `internal/mcpserver/{mcp.go,tools.go}` + `_test.go`.
- `cmd/serve.go`, `cmd/mcp.go` (thin cobra wrappers).

**Refactor steps (Phase 0, pure move, no behavior change):**

1. Move these from `cmd/build.go` → `internal/appcore/build.go`, exporting each: `buildEngine`→`BuildEngine`, `buildAutoChain`→`BuildAutoChain`, `llmFromProvider`→`LLMFromProvider`, `googleFromConfig`→`GoogleFromConfig`, `dictFromConfig`→`DictFromConfig`, `smartDictFromConfig`→`SmartDictFromConfig`, `smartAutoFromConfig`→`SmartAutoFromConfig`, `learnEngineFromConfig`→`LearnEngineFromConfig`. They already take `config.Resolved`/`*config.Config` only — no signature changes.
2. Move `toRecord` (`cmd/root.go:364`) → `appcore.ToRecord`; move the body of `effectiveTarget` (`cmd/root.go:268`, which calls `lang.PairTarget`) into an unexported `appcore.effectiveTarget` (drop the `debug.Logf` line, or keep `internal/debug` — it's dependency-free).
3. Add `appcore.DefineEngine(res config.Resolved, plain, smart bool) engine.Engine` capturing the selection logic in `cmd/define.go:72` (`defineEngine`) so CLI and Service agree on the exact same define engine.
4. Update call sites (the only churn):
   - `cmd/build.go`: keep `buildEngineSet` (it imports `tui`); rewrite its body to call `appcore.GoogleFromConfig/DictFromConfig/SmartDictFromConfig`.
   - `cmd/root.go:191` `buildEngine`→`appcore.BuildEngine`; `:402/:206/:248/:409` `learnEngineFromConfig`/`llmFromProvider`→`appcore.*`; `recordAndRemember` (`:356`) uses `appcore.ToRecord`.
   - `cmd/define.go`: `defineEngine` becomes a one-liner `return appcore.DefineEngine(res, flagDefinePlain, flagDefineSmart)`.
5. `just check` (`go vet . ./cmd/... ./internal/...` + build) must stay green. This phase ships alone with identical CLI behavior.

---

## 2. Shared service core (`internal/appcore.Service`)

Builds the engine **once** (warm) and holds the store for the process lifetime. One instance is shared across all HTTP handler goroutines and the MCP loop.

```go
type Service struct {
    cfg     *config.Config
    res     config.Resolved   // resolved once at startup (no per-request flags)
    trans   engine.Engine     // warm translate engine (BuildEngine)
    define  engine.Engine     // warm define engine (DefineEngine)
    store   store.Store       // shared history; nil when disabled/--no-history
    home    string            // resolved default target (pair "home")
    away    string            // resolved pair_with ("away"); "" when pair off
    pair    bool
}

type Options struct { NoHistory bool }

// NewService resolves cfg with empty overrides, warms both engines, opens the store.
func NewService(cfg *config.Config, opt Options) (*Service, error)

// newService(trans, define engine.Engine, st store.Store, ...) — unexported ctor for tests (no network).

type Params struct {
    Text, Source, Target, Preset, Instructions, Model string
    MaxAlts int
    Pair    *bool   // nil =&gt; use config default; non-nil =&gt; per-request override
}

func (s *Service) Translate(ctx context.Context, p Params) (*engine.TranslateResult, error)
func (s *Service) TranslateStream(ctx context.Context, p Params, onToken func(string)) (*engine.TranslateResult, error)
func (s *Service) Define(ctx context.Context, word string) (*engine.TranslateResult, error)
func (s *Service) HistoryRecent(ctx context.Context, limit int) ([]store.Record, error)
func (s *Service) HistorySearch(ctx context.Context, query string, limit int) ([]store.Record, error)
func (s *Service) Close() error   // closes the store
```

Internals — `Translate`/`TranslateStream` share a `buildRequest(p)` helper that mirrors `oneShot` (`cmd/root.go:462`) and `effectiveTarget` (`:268`):
- default `Source` ← `s.res.Source` (usually `"auto"`), `Target` ← `p.Target` or `s.home`;
- when pair is on, `req.Target = appcore.effectiveTarget(home, away, text)` and set `req.Pair/PairHome/PairAway` (so the LLM prompt never echoes input) exactly as the CLI does;
- pass `Preset`, `Extra=Instructions`, `Model`, `MaxAlts`, `Stream` through;
- call `s.trans.Translate(ctx, req)`, then `engine.Drain(ch, onToken)` (reuse the existing helper at `engine.go:225`);
- on success **and** `!Truncated`, record via `s.store.Add(ctx, appcore.ToRecord(res, text, source, target))`. Do **not** call `saveLastPair` — the server must stay out of `state.json` so it never fights the CLI's remembered pair.

`Define` calls `s.define.Translate(ctx, engine.Request{Text: word, Mode: engine.ModeDict})` then `Drain(ch, nil)` — identical to `runDefine` (`cmd/define.go:44`) minus printing.

Key reuse: nothing here re-implements engine logic; it re-wires the exact CLI code paths (`Drain`, `ToRecord`, `effectiveTarget`, `buildRequest≈oneShot`).

---

## 3. HTTP server (`translate serve`)

**Router:** stdlib `net/http.ServeMux` with Go 1.22+ method+path patterns (`mux.HandleFunc("POST /v1/translate", …)`) — no framework, consistent with the codebase.

**cobra command** `cmd/serve.go` → `newServeCmd()`, registered in `cmd/root.go:95`. Flags (override `[server]` config): `--port int`, `--bind string`, `--token string`, `--no-history bool`, `--enable-mcp bool` (Phase 5). RunE:
```
cfg,_ ,_ := config.Load()
sc := cfg.ResolveServer(serveFlags)      // small helper: flags &gt; [server] &gt; defaults
svc,_ := appcore.NewService(cfg, appcore.Options{NoHistory: sc.NoHistory})
srv := server.New(svc, sc)               // *http.Server with the mux
run(cmd.Context(), srv, svc)             // graceful shutdown
```

**Endpoints** (reusing `engine.TranslateResult` — already fully JSON-tagged, `engine.go:72` — as the response body verbatim):

| Method + path | Request JSON | Response |
|---|---|---|
| `POST /v1/translate` | `{text, source?, target?, preset?, instructions?, model?, max_alts?, pair?}` | `TranslateResult` |
| `POST /v1/define` | `{word}` | `TranslateResult` (with `dictionary`/`suggestions`) |
| `GET /v1/history?q=&amp;limit=` | — | `[]store.Record` |
| `GET /v1/translate/stream?text=&amp;target=…` | query params (EventSource is GET-only) | `text/event-stream` |
| `GET /openapi.json`, `GET /docs`, `GET /healthz` | — | spec / Swagger UI / `{"status":"ok"}` |

Handlers decode with `json.NewDecoder(r.Body).Decode`, validate (empty `text`→422), call the `Service`, and `json.NewEncoder(w).Encode(res)`. History is guarded by the bearer token (section 7).

**SSE handler** (`internal/server/sse.go`):
```go
flusher, ok := w.(http.Flusher)            // 503 if not
w.Header().Set("Content-Type", "text/event-stream")
w.Header().Set("Cache-Control", "no-cache")
ctx := r.Context()                          // cancels on client disconnect
onToken := func(t string){ writeSSE(w,"token",t); flusher.Flush() }
res, err := svc.TranslateStream(ctx, params, onToken)
if err != nil { writeSSE(w,"error", jsonErr(err)); flusher.Flush(); return }
if res.Truncated { writeSSE(w,"warning","result was truncated (max_tokens)") }
writeSSE(w,"done", mustJSON(res)); flusher.Flush()
```
- **Client-disconnect cancellation is already wired end-to-end**: `r.Context()` → `Service` ctx → `engine.Engine.Translate(ctx,…)` → the LLM goroutine's `case &lt;-ctx.Done()` (confirmed at `internal/engine/llm.go:612` and `:665`), which aborts the upstream request. No extra plumbing needed.
- **Truncated warning preservation**: `TranslateResult.Truncated` is `json:"-"` (transient, `engine.go:88`), so it would vanish in the `done` payload — the handler must emit it as an explicit `warning` event, as above.
- **Streaming caveat (honor it, don't over-promise):** copilot-proxy buffers Claude `/v1/messages`, so visible token cadence stays coarse for Claude models regardless of SSE — this is upstream and transport-independent. Document it on `/docs` and in the backlog note; SSE's real win is dict/google/OpenAI-style providers and multi-client fan-out.

**Error model:** one envelope `{"error":{"code":"bad_request","message":"…"}}` via `writeError(w, status, code, msg)` in `errors.go`. Status map: 400 malformed JSON · 422 empty text/word · 401 missing/invalid token · 404 unknown route · 405 wrong method · 500 engine error. `engine.ErrEmptyInput` (`llm.go:188`) maps to 422.

**Graceful shutdown:** reuse the already-cancellable `cmd.Context()` (main wires `signal.NotifyContext(…, os.Interrupt)` at `main.go:15`; add `syscall.SIGTERM` in the serve command via its own `signal.NotifyContext`). On `ctx.Done()`: `srv.Shutdown(shutCtx)` with a ~5 s timeout, then `svc.Close()`. Log the bound address on startup.

---

## 4. OpenAPI + Swagger UI

**Recommendation: hand-written `/openapi.json` + `go:embed`-ded Swagger UI.** This matches the backlog's explicit preference and the zero-framework philosophy. Reject `swaggo/swag` (needs code annotations + a `go generate` step + a build-time binary) and `huma` (a full framework that would restructure every handler and add a large dep tree — contrary to the lean `go.mod`).

- **Spec:** author an OpenAPI 3.1 document as `internal/server/openapi.json`, `go:embed`-ded and served at `GET /openapi.json` with `Content-Type: application/json`. Keep the `components.schemas` for `TranslateResult`/`DictEntry`/`Record` in sync by hand (small surface; a `TestOpenAPISchemaMatchesStruct` reflection test can guard the top-level field names against the structs to catch drift).
- **Swagger UI:** vendor a pinned `swagger-ui-dist` (only `swagger-ui.css`, `swagger-ui-bundle.js`, and a small `index.html` whose `SwaggerUIBundle({url:'/openapi.json'})`) under `internal/server/swagger/`, `go:embed`-ded and served at `GET /docs` via `http.FileServerFS`. No CDN (loopback/offline-friendly). Note: adds ~1.5 MB to the binary; if that matters, gate behind a `swaggerui` build tag with a plain-text fallback at `/docs`.

---

## 5. MCP server (`translate mcp`)

**Recommendation: add a separate `translate mcp` stdio subcommand, and hand-roll JSON-RPC 2.0 over stdio (no SDK) for v1.**

Rationale:
- **Transport — stdio first.** Claude Desktop/Code/Cursor *spawn the binary* and speak newline-delimited JSON-RPC over stdio. This is the highest-value integration and it **sidesteps the entire HTTP security surface** (no port, no bind, no token; personal history never leaves the process's stdio pipe; the child inherits the user's env for provider keys). Streamable-HTTP `/mcp` on the serve port is a *later* add (Phase 5), gated by `[server].enable_mcp`, reusing the same tool handlers.
- **SDK vs hand-roll.** Only 3 tools, all mapping 1:1 to `Service` methods. The MCP stdio handshake is small and well-specified: `initialize` → `initialized` (notification) → `tools/list` → `tools/call`, framed as newline-delimited JSON-RPC 2.0. Hand-rolling is ~200–300 lines, adds **zero dependencies**, and mirrors the deliberate "hand-written OpenAPI" decision — consistent with the lean `go.mod` (no MCP dep today). Trade-off stated honestly: the official `github.com/modelcontextprotocol/go-sdk` handles protocol-version negotiation, content typing, resources/prompts/sampling, and future spec churn for you; hand-rolling risks spec-drift as MCP evolves. Mitigation: keep the JSON-RPC/dispatch layer isolated in `internal/mcpserver/mcp.go` so swapping in the official SDK later is a contained change. `mark3labs/mcp-go` is not recommended (community SDK, heavier dep tree, no advantage over the official SDK if we ever take a dep).

**Package `internal/mcpserver`:**
- `mcp.go`: JSON-RPC types; `Serve(ctx, in io.Reader, out io.Writer, svc *appcore.Service)` running a `bufio.Scanner` read-loop (one JSON message per line) → `dispatch(method, params)` → write response. Making it take `io.Reader/io.Writer` (not hard-coded os.Stdin/os.Stdout) is what enables the smoke test in section 9.
- `tools.go`: the tool registry + handlers.

**Tool schemas** (JSON Schema `inputSchema`; each handler calls the shared `Service`):

| Tool | inputSchema | Returns (`content: [{type:"text",…}]`) |
|---|---|---|
| `translate` | `{text*:string, target?:string, source?:string}` | translation text; alternatives/notes appended. Optionally `structuredContent` = the `TranslateResult` JSON. |
| `define` | `{word*:string}` | plain-formatted definition (or the `TranslateResult` JSON as text) |
| `history` | `{query?:string, limit?:integer}` | recent/matching records as a compact text list |

For define/history text, returning the `TranslateResult`/`[]Record` **JSON as a text block** is the pragmatic v1 choice (LLM clients parse it fine) — avoids pulling `renderDict` (`cmd/define.go:84`) out of `cmd`. If richer prose is wanted later, move a plain renderer into `appcore`.

`cmd/mcp.go` → `newMcpCmd()`: `RunE` builds `appcore.NewService(cfg, {})` then `mcpserver.Serve(cmd.Context(), os.Stdin, os.Stdout, svc)`. Registered at `cmd/root.go:95`. **Do not** write logs to stdout (it's the JSON-RPC channel) — send any diagnostics to stderr.

---

## 6. Config schema (`[server]` table)

Add to `Config` (`internal/config/config.go:17`):
```go
Server Server `toml:"server"`

type Server struct {
    Port      int    `toml:"port"`                 // default 4155
    Bind      string `toml:"bind"`                 // default "127.0.0.1"
    Token     string `toml:"token,omitempty"`      // optional inline bearer token
    TokenEnv  string `toml:"token_env,omitempty"`  // env var holding the token (preferred)
    EnableMCP bool   `toml:"enable_mcp"`           // expose /mcp (Phase 5); default false
}
```
In `Default()` (`config.go:178`): `Server: Server{Port: 4155, Bind: "127.0.0.1"}`. **Bump `SchemaVersion` 1→2** (`config.go:43`) with a history comment (`2 — [server] table (translate serve)`). No migration code required: the auto-upgrade path at `cmd/root.go:146-158` already re-saves and materializes additive defaults ("Schema bumps so far are additive…"). Add a small `Config.ResolveServer(flags)` helper (flags &gt; `[server]` &gt; defaults) used by `cmd/serve.go`.

---

## 7. Security

- **Loopback bind is mandatory and fail-closed.** Default `Bind:"127.0.0.1"`. If a resolved bind is non-loopback (not `127.0.0.0/8`/`::1`), **refuse to start** unless a token is configured (require intent + auth before exposing personal history on a LAN). Enforce with a `net.ParseIP(...).IsLoopback()` check in `server.New`.
- **Bearer token.** Resolve token from `--token` &gt; `[server].token` &gt; `os.Getenv([server].token_env)` (recommend `token_env` so the secret stays out of `config.toml`). When set, require `Authorization: Bearer &lt;token&gt;` (constant-time compare via `crypto/subtle.ConstantTimeCompare`) on **`/v1/history`** always (personal data). Optionally guard all `/v1/*` when a `require_token_all` intent is desired; `/healthz`, `/openapi.json`, `/docs` stay open on loopback.
- **MCP stdio sidesteps all of this**: no listener, no port, no token — the transport is a private stdio pipe to a parent process the user already trusts, and history never crosses a network boundary. This is the security argument for shipping MCP as stdio first.

---

## 8. Incremental delivery / phasing

- **Phase 0 — Refactor to `internal/appcore` + `Service` (no behavior change).** Ships alone; de-risks everything; existing CLI byte-identical. Gated by `just check`/`just test`.
- **Phase 1 — `translate serve` REST JSON** (`/v1/translate`, `/v1/define`, `/v1/history`, `/healthz`) + `[server]` config + loopback enforcement + token + graceful shutdown. Ships usable (Raycast/Alfred/curl can hit JSON).
- **Phase 2 — SSE** `/v1/translate/stream`. Independent increment on top of Phase 1.
- **Phase 3 — OpenAPI + Swagger** `/openapi.json` + `/docs`. Pure add.
- **Phase 4 — `translate mcp` (stdio, hand-rolled).** **Depends only on Phase 0**, *not* on Phases 1–3 — can ship in parallel with, or even before, the HTTP work. This is worth calling out: MCP is the cheapest high-value deliverable once `Service` exists.
- **Phase 5 (optional) — `/mcp` streamable-HTTP** on the serve port, gated by `[server].enable_mcp`, reusing Phase 4's tool handlers.

Independently shippable: Phase 0, Phase 4 (given 0), and each of 1/2/3 layer additively.

---

## 9. Testing strategy

- **`appcore.Service`** (`service_test.go`): table tests with a **stub `engine.Engine`** returning canned `Chunk`s (translate: tokens + `ChunkDone`; define: single `ChunkDone` with a `Dictionary`; error: `ChunkError`). Construct via the unexported `newService(stubEng, stubDef, memStore, cfg)`. Assert target routing (pair on/off via `lang.PairTarget`), history recording, and `Truncated` suppression of the record. Use a temp-file `store.OpenJSONL(t.TempDir()+"/h.jsonl")` for real store round-trips.
- **HTTP handlers** (`internal/server/*_test.go`): follow the existing `httptest` pattern already used in `internal/engine/llm_test.go` (`httptest.NewServer`, line 14). For JSON endpoints use `httptest.NewRecorder` + the mux; assert status + decoded body. For token: assert 401 without header, 200 with.
- **SSE**: `httptest.ResponseRecorder` implements `http.Flusher`, so a recorder + stub engine that emits N tokens lets you assert the body contains `event: token` × N and a terminal `event: done`; add one `httptest.NewServer` test that reads the response body line-by-line to exercise real flushing and a `r.Context()`-cancel path (cancel the client, assert the stub's ctx fired).
- **MCP** (`internal/mcpserver/mcp_test.go`): drive `Serve(ctx, in, out, svc)` with `in := bytes.NewBufferString(...)` containing the `initialize` → `tools/list` → `tools/call` line sequence and a `bytes.Buffer` out; assert the handshake result advertises 3 tools and that `tools/call` returns the stubbed translation. Table-test each tool via `dispatch`.
- **Coverage/CI**: all three new packages sit under `internal/`, so the existing globs (`go test ./cmd/... ./internal/...`, `go vet …` — `Justfile` lines 36/44) pick them up with **no Justfile change**. `cmd/serve.go`/`cmd/mcp.go` stay thin (logic lives in `internal/`), consistent with `cmd` having no tests today.

---

## 10. Risks / open questions to surface

1. **Cross-process history writes (server + CLI).** `store/jsonl.go` uses an in-process `sync.Mutex` only (line 20), no flock. Two processes `O_APPEND` to `history.jsonl`: POSIX guarantees atomic appends only under `PIPE_BUF` (4096 B) — long inputs could interleave. Options: (a) accept it and document (clients that use the server don't also spawn the CLI, so collisions are rare); (b) **harden `jsonlStore.Add`** with an advisory `syscall.Flock(fd, LOCK_EX)` on the open file (unix), behind a build tag with a Windows no-op. Recommend (b) as a small, contained follow-up; needs your call since it touches a shared file. `Recent`/`Search` re-read the whole file each call (`readAll`, line 61) — fine for personal-scale history, but note it's O(file) per request under a server.
2. **Per-request engine/provider switching is out of scope for v1.** The Service warms **one** engine at startup; per-request overrides are limited to what the warm engine already honors via `engine.Request` (source, target, preset, instructions, `model`/`model_provider`, max_alts). Confirm that's acceptable, or we add an engine cache keyed by `{engine,provider,model}` later.
3. **copilot-proxy buffering** caps *visible* Claude streaming regardless of SSE (upstream). Set expectations on `/docs`.
4. **MCP spec drift** with hand-rolled JSON-RPC vs taking the official Go SDK dep — accepted for v1 (isolated in `mcp.go` for easy later swap). Confirm the minimal-dep stance holds for MCP.
5. **Daemon lifecycle is explicitly out of scope** (backlog open question): the binary offers `serve`; keeping it running (LaunchAgent / menu-bar / extension auto-start) is separate and re-introduces the first-spawn PATH problem. Not solved here.
6. **Port 4155 default** — collision risk; no discovery mechanism in v1 (config'd default per the backlog).
7. **Bilingual / learn have no API/MCP surface in v1** (structured, non-streaming, CLI-shaped). Note as future.
8. **Token in `config.toml`** (0600) vs `token_env` — recommend `token_env`; confirm.
9. **Swagger UI binary-size** (~1.5 MB embedded) — acceptable, or gate behind a build tag?

---

These two questions—how to implement MCP (SDK vs. handwritten vs. defer) and how to expose it (stdio subcommand vs. HTTP on the serve port vs. both)—would fully nail down the MCP architecture, which is exactly where their uncertainty lies. Options for Q1 are shaping up: stdio subcommand (cleanest for Claude Desktop/Code/Cursor, no open port or token overhead), HTTP /mcp on the serve port (bundles everything but adds daemon lifecycle complexity), both approaches (most flexible but more work), or don't include MCP at all.

For Q2, if we do build MCP, I'm weighing the official Go SDK (stable, maintained, handles protocol negotiation and spec compliance) against a hand-rolled JSON-RPC approach (keeps dependencies minimal, ~250 lines for the three tools, but means owning the spec compliance ourselves). I'm leaning toward the official SDK for maintainability.

These two questions are genuinely load-bearing for the architecture — they'll determine package structure, dependencies, and security surface — so it's worth asking them now. Then I can incorporate the answers into the final plan and exit planning mode.

Before I ask though, I should validate the design by reading some of the critical files myself rather than relying entirely on agents. Let me start with the questions first since they're independent, then I'll read through cmd/build.go, internal/engine/engine.go, and the relevant sections of cmd/root.go to confirm the refactor approach and the [server] table plan. I need to read through the config schema, root command setup, and engine internals to validate the design before asking clarifying questions—I'll batch these file reads together to be efficient.
</details></think>

```text
1	package cmd
2	
3	import (
4		"fmt"
5		"time"
6	
7		"github.com/daviddwlee84/translate/internal/config"
8		"github.com/daviddwlee84/translate/internal/engine"
9		"github.com/daviddwlee84/translate/internal/tui"
10	)
11	
12	// buildEngine constructs the Engine for an invocation from resolved settings.
13	// engine="auto" builds a fallback Chain over the providers named in chain.order;
14	// engine="smartauto" builds the smart default (dictionary for words, LLM for
15	// phrases, bidirectional); a named provider builds a single LLM engine.
16	func buildEngine(res config.Resolved) (engine.Engine, error) {
17		cfg := res.Cfg
18	
19		switch res.Engine {
20		case "auto":
21			return buildAutoChain(res)
22		case "smartauto":
23			// Needs an LLM provider for the word-miss fallback and phrase translation;
24			// without one, behave like the plain auto chain (google/dict).
25			if res.Provider == nil {
26				return buildAutoChain(res)
27			}
28			return smartAutoFromConfig(res), nil
29		case "google":
30			return googleFromConfig(cfg), nil
31		case "llm":
32			// "llm" means "the resolved provider" (already chosen by Resolve).
33			if res.Provider != nil {
34				return llmFromProvider(res.Provider, res.Model), nil
35			}
36			return nil, fmt.Errorf("unknown engine/provider %q (check %s)", res.Engine, config.Path())
37		default:
38			// A named provider (e.g. "copilot", "ollama").
39			if res.Provider != nil {
40				return llmFromProvider(res.Provider, res.Model), nil
41			}
42			return nil, fmt.Errorf("unknown engine/provider %q (check %s)", res.Engine, config.Path())
43		}
44	}
45	
46	// buildAutoChain builds the "auto" fallback Chain over the providers/google named
47	// in chain.order. (google/dict engines otherwise join only via smart-auto or the
48	// TUI ^e cycle.)
49	func buildAutoChain(res config.Resolved) (engine.Engine, error) {
50		cfg := res.Cfg
51		var engines []engine.Engine
52		for _, name := range cfg.Chain.Order {
53			switch {
54			case cfg.ProviderByName(name) != nil:
55				p := cfg.ProviderByName(name)
56				engines = append(engines, llmFromProvider(p, p.ModelForTier(res.Tier)))
57			case name == "google" && cfg.Google.Enabled:
58				engines = append(engines, googleFromConfig(cfg))
59			}
60			// "dict" is wired in via smart-auto / the TUI engine cycle.
61		}
62		if len(engines) == 0 {
63			return nil, fmt.Errorf("no engines available in chain.order (check %s)", config.Path())
64		}
65		if len(engines) == 1 {
66			return engines[0], nil
67		}
68		return engine.NewChain(engines, 5*time.Second), nil
69	}
70	
71	// llmFromProvider builds an LLM engine for a provider using the given model id.
72	func llmFromProvider(p *config.Provider, model string) *engine.LLMEngine {
73		if model == "" {
74			model = p.Model
75		}
76		return engine.NewLLM(engine.LLMConfig{
77			Name:      p.Name,
78			BaseURL:   p.BaseURL,
79			Model:     model,
80			APIKeyEnv: p.APIKeyEnv,
81		})
82	}
83	
84	// googleFromConfig builds the free Google engine from config.
85	func googleFromConfig(cfg *config.Config) *engine.GoogleEngine {
86		return engine.NewGoogle(engine.GoogleConfig{
87			Endpoint:  cfg.Google.Endpoint,
88			ExtraDT:   cfg.Google.ExtraDT,
89			UserAgent: cfg.Google.UserAgent,
90			Timeout:   time.Duration(cfg.Google.TimeoutMs) * time.Millisecond,
91		})
92	}
93	
94	// dictFromConfig builds the dictionary engine from config: the offline bilingual
95	// engine (source=local, default) or the dictionaryapi.dev engine (source=api).
96	func dictFromConfig(cfg *config.Config) engine.Engine {
97		if cfg.Dict.Source == "api" {
98			return engine.NewDict(engine.DictConfig{
99				Endpoint: cfg.Dict.Endpoint,
100				Lang:     cfg.Dict.Lang,
101				Fuzzy:    cfg.Dict.Fuzzy,
102				Wordlist: cfg.Dict.Wordlist,
103			})
104		}
105		var apiFb *engine.DictEngine
106		if cfg.Dict.APIFallback {
107			apiFb = engine.NewDict(engine.DictConfig{
108				Endpoint: cfg.Dict.Endpoint,
109				Lang:     cfg.Dict.Lang,
110				Fuzzy:    cfg.Dict.Fuzzy,
111				Wordlist: cfg.Dict.Wordlist,
112			})
113		}
114		return engine.NewLocalDict(engine.LocalDictConfig{
115			Dir:          cfg.Dict.Dir,
116			CedictURL:    cfg.Dict.CedictURL,
117			EcdictURL:    cfg.Dict.EcdictURL,
118			AutoDownload: cfg.Dict.AutoDownload,
119			Fuzzy:        cfg.Dict.Fuzzy,
120			APIFallback:  apiFb,
121		})
122	}
123	
124	// smartDictFromConfig builds the smart-dict engine: the plain dictionary plus an
125	// LLM fallback (resolved provider/model) for misses and too-weak fuzzy matches.
126	// The caller must ensure res.Provider != nil.
127	func smartDictFromConfig(res config.Resolved) engine.Engine {
128		cfg := res.Cfg
129		return engine.NewSmartDict(dictFromConfig(cfg), llmFromProvider(res.Provider, res.Model), engine.SmartDictConfig{
130			CloseDistance: cfg.SmartDict.CloseDistance,
131			Preset:        cfg.SmartDict.Preset,
132		})
133	}
134	
135	// smartAutoFromConfig builds the smart-auto default: a single word/term is a
136	// dictionary lookup (smart-dict, with an LLM fallback), and a phrase is an LLM
137	// translation via the auto chain (so phrases keep provider→google resilience).
138	// The caller must ensure res.Provider != nil.
139	func smartAutoFromConfig(res config.Resolved) engine.Engine {
140		llm, err := buildAutoChain(res)
141		if err != nil {
142			// No chain engines (unlikely once a provider exists): fall back to the bare
143			// resolved provider so phrases can still translate.
144			llm = llmFromProvider(res.Provider, res.Model)
145		}
146		return engine.NewSmartAuto(smartDictFromConfig(res), llm)
147	}
148	
149	// learnEngineFromConfig builds the engine for learning mode: a bare LLM engine
150	// (the resolved provider/model), bypassing smart-auto/dictionary routing so a
151	// single word still gets the structured tutor treatment. Caller ensures Provider != nil.
152	func learnEngineFromConfig(res config.Resolved) engine.Engine {
153		return llmFromProvider(res.Provider, res.Model)
154	}
155	
156	// buildEngineSet builds the list of engines the TUI can cycle through with ^e:
157	// the resolved primary (translate), plus Google (fast, keyless), the dictionary
158	// (word lookup), and — when an LLM provider is available — the smart dictionary
159	// (dictionary with an LLM fallback), so the user can switch on the fly.
160	func buildEngineSet(res config.Resolved, primary engine.Engine) []tui.NamedEngine {
161		cfg := res.Cfg
162		primaryName := primary.Name() // "auto", "smart-auto", "copilot", "google", …
163		if primaryName == "" {
164			primaryName = "auto"
165		}
166		set := []tui.NamedEngine{{Name: primaryName, Engine: primary, Mode: engine.ModeTranslate}}
167		if primaryName != "google" && cfg.Google.Enabled {
168			set = append(set, tui.NamedEngine{Name: "google", Engine: googleFromConfig(cfg), Mode: engine.ModeTranslate})
169		}
170		if cfg.Dict.Enabled {
171			set = append(set, tui.NamedEngine{Name: "dictionary", Engine: dictFromConfig(cfg), Mode: engine.ModeDict})
172			if res.Provider != nil {
173				set = append(set, tui.NamedEngine{Name: "smart-dict", Engine: smartDictFromConfig(res), Mode: engine.ModeDict})
174			}
175		}
176		return set
177	}
178
```
</details></tool-use>
